elements.sent-2411-001: Modern cyberattacks in cyber-physical systems (CPS) rapidly evolve and cannot be deterred effectively with most current methods which focused on characterizing past threats.
elements.sent-2411-002: Adaptive anomaly detection (AAD) is among the most promising techniques to detect evolving cyberattacks focused on fast data processing and model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-003: AAD has been researched in the literature extensively; however, to the best of our knowledge, our work is the first systematic literature review (SLR) on the current research within this field.
elements.sent-2411-004: We present a comprehensive SLR, gathering $397$ relevant papers and systematically analyzing $65$ of them (47 research and 18 survey papers) on AAD in CPS studies from 2013 to 2023 (November).
elements.sent-2411-005: We introduce a novel taxonomy considering attack types, CPS application, learning paradigm, data management, and algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our analysis indicates, among other findings, that reviewed works focused on a single aspect of adaptation (either data processing or model adaptation) but rarely in both at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-007: We aim to help researchers to advance the state of the art and help practitioners to become familiar with recent progress in this field.
elements.sent-2411-008: We identify the limitations of the state of the art and provide recommendations for future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-009: Modern cyber-physical systems (CPS) including industrial control systems (ICS), vehicles, power grids, and the Internet of Things (IoT), among others, generate vast amounts of high speed data that need to be processed to support decision-making capabilities~\cite{Atat:2018:Big:Data:CPS:Panoramic:Survey, Fei:2019:CPS:Stream:Analytics, Kayan:2022:Cybersecurity:Industrial:CPS .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Due to the mission-critical nature that CPS play, securing its operation against malicious threats is essential for guaranteeing daily life activities.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, since information technology and physical processes are closely linked in CPS, they face a broader range of threats, including both cyber and physical attacks.
elements.sent-2411-012: Here, cyberattacks refer to attacks against the communication and computing components of CPS~\cite{Shacham:2004:Effectiveness:Address:Space:Randomization, Pike:2016:Trackos:Security:Aware:Real:Time:OS, Clements:2017:Protecting:embedded:Systems:Privilige:Overlays .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Physical attacks refers to compromising the physical environment of a CPS, subjecting the system to potential malicious data via injection or tampering through sensor and/or actuators~\cite{Rutkin:2013:GPS:Spoofers:Yacth, Shoukry:2013:Non:Invasive:Spoofing:Attacks:Anti:Lock:Break, Petit:2015:Remote:Attacks:Vehicle:Sensors:Camera:Lidar .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Among the most used techniques to detect threats in CPS are those based on anomaly detection~\cite{Chandola:2009:Anomaly:Detection:Survey, Mirsky:2018:Kitsune, Moriano:2021:Burstiness:BGP, Moriano:2022:AHC .}
elements.sent-2411-015: As opposed to traditional signature-based detection focused on matching patterns from previously seen attacks~\cite{Hubballi:2014:False:Alarm:Minimization:Techniques:Signature:IDS, Ioulianou:2018:Signature:Based:IDS:IoT, Wu:2019:Survey:Intursion:Detection:Vehicle:Networks , anomaly detection techniques focus on spotting behavior that looks different from the expected norm~\cite{Luo:2021:DL:Anomaly:Detection:CPS, Shahriar:2023:CANshield, Moriano:2024:Benchmarking:Unsupervised:Online:IDS .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This approach helps on identifying previously unseen attacks as those commonly affecting the cyber and physical components of CPS~\cite{Schneider:2018:High:Performance:Unsupervised:Anomaly:Detection:CPS, Xi:2022:Data:Correlation:Aware:Unsupervised:DL:Anomaly:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Different anomaly detection approaches have been proposed to secure CPS including those based on attack-resilient sensor fusion~\cite{Ivanov:2016:Attack:Resilient:Sensor:Fusion:CPS, Lu:2018:Attack:Resilient:Sesnor:Fusion:Adaptive:Cruise:Control , model-based attack detection~\cite{Quinonez:2020:SAVIOR:Securing:Autonomous:Vehicles:Robust:Physical:Invariants, Giraldo:2018:Survey:Physics:Based:Attack:CPS , and data-based detection~\cite{Junejo:2016:Behaviour:attack:Detection:CPS:ML, Shin:2017:Intelligent:Sensor:Attack:Detection:Automobile .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the ability of anomaly detection methods to adapt to detect previously unseen attacks is usually not thoroughly explored.
elements.sent-2411-019: Here, adaptability is closely tied with the concept of ``adaptation,'' which means the ability of an anomaly detection method to anticipate and respond to new and emerging security threats by learning from the experience and the current state of the CPS~\cite{Abie:2019:Cognitive:Cyber:CPS:IoT, Andrade:2019:Cognitive:Security .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Adaptive anomaly detection (AAD) requires two key components: (1) near real-time data processing and (2) a predefined learning mode for model adaptation~\cite{Raciti:2013:Anomaly:And:Adaptation:CPS, Settanni:2018:Protecting:CPS:AD:Adaptation, Akowuah:2021:Adaptive:Sensor:Attack:Autonomous:CPS, Biggio:2024:ML:Security:Difficult:Fix .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Near real-time processing assists in detecting attacks before they cause consequences, which is crucial in safety-critical CPS.
elements.sent-2411-022: In addition, a predefined learning model (such as full, incremental, or hybrid retrain~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift ) is needed to adapt the detection model to respond better to unseen attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Both components of AAD generally ensure a strong defense against advanced cyberattacks.
elements.sent-2411-024: This paper presents a systematic literature review (SLR) on AAD in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of the state of the art in AAD, including their usage across different types of CPS, classification across different learning paradigms, common algorithms, and a discussion of trends and gaps in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-026: To our knowledge, this is the first SLR on AAD in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-027: More specifically, the objectives of our SLR are to: (1) provide researchers with an understanding of current AAD methods in CPS, enabling new researchers to quickly familiarize themselves; (2) highlight gaps and opportunities for future research; and (3) support practitioners in selecting and adapting AAD methods in CPS to fit their needs.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the past decade, a large number of studies were published covering different aspects of AAD in CPS~\cite{Li:2016:Dirichlet:Based:Detection:Smart:Grid, Adhikari:2017:Applying:Adaptive:Threes:Real:Time:IDS, Van:2019:Real:Time:Anomaly:Detection:Automated:Vehicles, Yasaei:2020:IoT:CAD:Context:Aware:Anomaly:Detection:Through:Sensor:Association, Mowla:2020:AFRL:Adaptive:Federated:RL:Jamming, Jiao:2022:Cyberattack:Resilient:Forecasting:Adaptive:Robust:Regression, Ding:2022:Data:Driven:Situational:Awarness:Power:systems, Gyamfi:2022:Novel:Online:IDS:IoT:OI-SVDD:AS-ELM, intriago2023real, cai2023adam .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Nonetheless, most studies investigated focus on only one of the key components of AAD (i.e., near real-time data processing or predefined learning mode).
elements.sent-2411-030: Accordingly, results were contradictory and practices heterogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-031: This makes it difficult to contextualize their contribution in terms of how adaptation is carried out.
elements.sent-2411-032: To fill in the gaps and provide an updated and comprehensive review on the latest developments in AAD in CPS, the present study reviews state-of-the-art works published between 2013 and 2023 (November).
elements.sent-2411-033: The contributions of this article can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-034: We review and classify state-of-the-art AAD methods in CPS considering type of application, learning paradigm, data management strategy, and algorithms, along with a comprehensive summary tables (see Tables~\ref{table: Adaptive anomaly detection in ICS applications -\ref{table: Adaptive anomaly detection in smart grids ).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We introduce a novel AAD taxonomy for CPS that focuses of attack types, applications, and ML algorithms to categorize reviewed works based on learning paradigm and algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-036: We identify and discuss limitations of reviewed AAD approaches for securing CPS.
elements.sent-2411-037: We discuss priority future areas of research in this field.
elements.sent-2411-038: We organize this SLR as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: In Section~\ref{sec:related work , we contextualize our SLR with respect to other surveys in closely-related areas.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Section~\ref{sec:Methods details the methodology we used to conduct the SLR.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{sec:Results introduces an AAD taxonomy for CPS and synthesize previous research based on the learning paradigm of the algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section~\ref{sec:discussion , we discuss our findings and potential future research directions.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Finally, we provide a brief summary of this SLR in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-044: As the field of AAD in CPS has matured over the last decade, several survey papers have been published with both broad and narrow scope in this field.
elements.sent-2411-045: Saad et al.~\cite{Saad:2019:Review:Mitigation:Attacks:SmartGrids analyzed cybersecurity challenges in smart grids.}
elements.sent-2411-046: They examined vulnerabilities, security needs, detection techniques, countermeasures, and secure communication protocols.
elements.sent-2411-047: Their study emphasized integrating advanced communication and computing technologies to improve reliability and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-048: They proposed solutions to mitigate cyberattacks, enhancing the security of power networks.
elements.sent-2411-049: Meng et al.~\cite{Zhang:2019:Review:FDI:Against:SG:Estimation summarized recent advances in false data injection attacks (FDIA) targeting smart grid state estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-050: They reviewed FDIA construction methods, detection strategies, and defense mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-051: They also outlined future directions, such as applying FDIAs to alternating current (AC) state estimation and using data-driven models for FDIA detection.
elements.sent-2411-052: Syrmakesis et al.~\cite{Syrmakesis:2022:Classifying:Resilience:Approaches:Smart:Grid classified cyber-resilience methods for protecting smart grids.}
elements.sent-2411-053: They reviewed approaches addressing cyberattacks and anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-054: Their taxonomy supports research on cyber-resilience and identifies promising directions for smart grid security.
elements.sent-2411-055: Rojas and Rauch~\cite{Rojas:2019:Review:Enablers:Smart:Manufacturing conducted a systematic review of 165 papers on CPS production trends.}
elements.sent-2411-056: They highlighted the importance of connectivity and control systems in manufacturing.
elements.sent-2411-057: Their work grouped papers into six categories, including cybersecurity enablers for smart manufacturing.
elements.sent-2411-058: Zeadally et al.~\cite{Zeadally:2019:Self:Adaptation:CPS reviewed 12 self-adaptive approaches for managing large-scale CPS.}
elements.sent-2411-059: They discussed the strengths, weaknesses, and implementation techniques for self-adaptive mechanisms across CPS architectural layers, including physical, network, and cyber.
elements.sent-2411-060: Rosenberg et al.~\cite{Rosenberg:2021:Adversarial:ML:Attacks:Defense:Cyber reviewed 58 papers on adversarial attacks and defenses in cybersecurity.}
elements.sent-2411-061: They proposed a taxonomy based on attack stages, goals, and capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-062: Their work highlighted future research needs in adversarial machine learning (ML).
elements.sent-2411-063: Jamal et al.~\cite{Jamal:2023:Review:Security:Analysis:CPS:ML reviewed ML and deep learning (DL) techniques for CPS security.}
elements.sent-2411-064: They identified challenges, reviewed existing methods, and proposed future directions for artificial intelligence (AI)-based CPS protection against cyber threats.
elements.sent-2411-065: Pekaric et al.~\cite{Pekaric:2023:Systematic:Security:Safety:Self:Adaptive:Systems reviewed 21 papers on safety and security in self-adaptive systems.}
elements.sent-2411-066: They found that current approaches rarely model both aspects together and often rely on simulations with simplified use cases.
elements.sent-2411-067: Koay et al.~\cite{Koay:2023:ML:ICS listed vulnerabilities and cyberattacks in ICS.}
elements.sent-2411-068: They compared ML methods for attack detection and highlighted challenges like limited datasets and risks of adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-070: Mahapatra et al.~\cite{Mahapatra:2020:Survey:IoT:Taxonomy:Challenges created a taxonomy for secure IoT communication.}
elements.sent-2411-071: They classified architectures, communication topologies, and secure transmission methods, including cluster-based and blockchain-based techniques.
elements.sent-2411-072: They identified research challenges and opportunities to enhance IoT security.
elements.sent-2411-073: Stoyanova et al.~\cite{Stoyanova:2020:Survey:IoT:Forensics reviewed IoT forensics and its challenges, such as device diversity, encryption, and cloud reliance.}
elements.sent-2411-074: They examined theoretical models and proposed frameworks using blockchain for evidence integrity.
elements.sent-2411-075: They emphasized the need for standardized forensic processes and readiness strategies.
elements.sent-2411-076: Dai and Boroomand~\cite{Dai:2022:Review:AI:Security:Big:Data reviewed 58 papers on security issues in big data systems.}
elements.sent-2411-077: They focused on AI techniques like DL and multi-agent systems for detecting and mitigating attacks.
elements.sent-2411-078: They mapped these methods to security strategies and evaluation models.
elements.sent-2411-079: Huang et al.~\cite{Huang:2022:RL:Feedback:Enabled:Cyber reviewed reinforcement learning (RL) for cyber-resilience.}
elements.sent-2411-080: They discussed vulnerabilities, including posture- and information-related issues, and applications like moving target defense.
elements.sent-2411-081: They also analyzed vulnerabilities in RL systems under adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-082: Alaghbari et al.~\cite{Alaghbari:2022:Complex:Event:Processing:Datacenter surveyed complex event processing (CEP) in CPS security.}
elements.sent-2411-083: They highlighted how combining CEP with ML enhances intrusion detection.
elements.sent-2411-084: They also discussed open issues in CEP applications for cybersecurity.
elements.sent-2411-085: Lin et al.~\cite{Lin:2023:Security:5G:IoT:Factories surveyed security and privacy issues in 5G-industrial IoT (IIoT) factories.}
elements.sent-2411-086: They reviewed approaches using DL, RL, and blockchain.
elements.sent-2411-087: Their work identified research opportunities for securing 5G-enabled industrial systems.
elements.sent-2411-088: Grim et al.~\cite{Grimm:2021:Context:Aware:Vehicles:Fleet reviewed 50 papers on adaptive and intelligent security for vehicles and fleets.}
elements.sent-2411-089: They identified open research areas and developed a taxonomy of contextual information categories.
elements.sent-2411-090: Their work aims to guide future developments in automotive security.
elements.sent-2411-091: Strandberg et al.~\cite{Strandberg:2022:Systematic:Review:Automotive:Digital:Forensics conducted a systematic review of 67 papers on automotive digital forensics.}
elements.sent-2411-092: They categorized papers into technical solutions and surveys and mapped forensic data to security properties and stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-093: Their findings are relevant to CPS like smart cities.
elements.sent-2411-094: Cooper et al.~\cite{Cooper:2023:Anomaly:Detection:Power:System:State:Estimation reviewed anomaly detection methods for power system state estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-095: They connected traditional data-driven methods with modern approaches addressing new cyber threats.
elements.sent-2411-096: They proposed directions for future research, including dynamic load profiles and asynchronous measurements.
elements.sent-2411-097: Table~\ref{table: related work lists and identifies prior surveys related to AAD in CPS.}
elements.sent-2411-098: It also outlines publication details and the contribution of existing surveys in the area.
elements.sent-2411-099: While there have been several valuable works reviewing different aspects of AAD in CPS, including adaptability~\cite{Zeadally:2019:Self:Adaptation:CPS, Pekaric:2023:Systematic:Security:Safety:Self:Adaptive:Systems and online learning~\cite{Rosenberg:2021:Adversarial:ML:Attacks:Defense:Cyber, Huang:2022:RL:Feedback:Enabled:Cyber , they do not provide an extensive analysis of other critical aspects related to these tasks including data management and concept drift.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, Zeadally et al.~\cite{Zeadally:2019:Self:Adaptation:CPS focuses on discussing techniques that enable self-adaptation capabilities within CPS at different architectural layers.}
elements.sent-2411-101: However, it lacks insight into the role of different data management strategies to handle streaming data conditions, as well as the categorization of adaptive techniques per application.
elements.sent-2411-102: To address these limitations and provide an in-depth understanding of state-of-the-art on AAD in CPS, we conduct a systematic and extensive survey of related literature.
elements.sent-2411-103: By comprehensively collecting $397$ papers and systematically analyzing $65$ papers (47 research papers and 18 surveys) from top journals and conferences, our SLR aims to provide a holistic summary of AAD methods and its broad applicability within the CPS domain.
elements.sent-2411-104: This approach enables a more nuanced analysis of the field highlighting strengths and limitations of different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-105: This contributes to identify most promising directions for future research.

elements.sent-2411-106: This section discusses the scope and SLR used in this work.
elements.sent-2411-107: We adapt Okoli's \cite{okoli2015guide review process for creating a standalone SLR and designed it with precision to ensure the comprehensive collection of relevant articles while maintaining methodological rigor.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Here, the review process consists of several sequential steps including planning, selection, extraction, and execution that ultimately organize the review process into a reproducible SLR.
elements.sent-2411-109: To maintain the integrity of this SLR and ensure that the selected articles met the requisite standards of relevance and quality, we establish and follow a strict inclusion and exclusion criteria.
elements.sent-2411-110: To be included in this SLR, studies had to:.
elements.sent-2411-111: Be published in peer-reviewed journals or conference proceedings, ensuring that only high-quality, validated research was considered.
elements.sent-2411-112: Be published within the past 10 years (2013–Nov.
elements.sent-2411-113: Be written in English to maintain consistency in interpretation and reduce potential translation biases.
elements.sent-2411-114: Address topics relevant to AAD or its subdomains with a clear focus on adaptive, cognitive, or autonomous mechanisms for anomaly detection in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-116: Were non-peer-reviewed articles, such as editorials, letters, etc..
elements.sent-2411-117: Lacked a clear connection to AAD or cognitive approaches for CPS, ensuring that the review remained focused on its central research question.
elements.sent-2411-118: This SLR was conducted by retrieving academic papers and conference proceedings from five prominent article databases: IEEE Xplore (\url{https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/ ), ACM Digital Library (\url{https://dl.acm.org/ ), Emerald Insight (\url{https://emerald.com/insight/ ), Springer Link (\url{https://link.springer.com/ ), and ScienceDirect (\url{https://sciencedirect.com/search ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: These databases were selected due to their extensive coverage of fields related to cybersecurity, adaptive systems, and AI, ensuring a comprehensive exploration of relevant literature.
elements.sent-2411-120: Each database was searched for publications covering the period from 2013 to November 2023 to align with the eligibility criteria.
elements.sent-2411-121: In addition to electronic searches within these databases, supplementary strategies (forward/backward search~\cite{wohlin2014guidelines ) were applied to identify additional key studies.}
elements.sent-2411-122: This approach enabled us to capture influential works not readily indexed by standard database searches and enhanced the comprehensiveness of our SLR.
elements.sent-2411-123: Figure \ref{fig:des-dia highlights the initial search process, which first establishes 10 key-terms including: adaptive cyber security; dynamic adaption AND cyber security; adaptive control AND cyber security; dynamic control AND cyber security; adaptive AND cyber threats; dynamic AND cyber threats; adaptive AND cognitive cybersecurity; intelligent cyber AND threat detection; cognitive cyber AND threat detection; Adaptive systems AND cognitive cyber.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Adjustments were made to improve search efficacy based on each database’s functionality.
elements.sent-2411-125: For example, the search term ``adaptive security'' was used in the ACM Digital Library to retrieve a broader set of results, while ScienceDirect required expanded criteria to title/abstract/author-specified keywords for higher relevancy.
elements.sent-2411-126: To enhance the comprehensiveness of the SLR, we performed a forward and backward search on a set of selected high-impact and high-quality papers.
elements.sent-2411-127: The set of core papers were used in congruence with a research analytics tool called SciVal from Scopus, which provided us with accurate and up-to-date citation numbers for each paper.
elements.sent-2411-128: After setting a threshold at the 99th percentile for citation numbers, we identified four papers that met or exceeded this criterion.
elements.sent-2411-129: These core papers became the cornerstone of our forward and backward search, allowing us to trace their references (backward search) and discover the subsequent papers that cited them (forward search).
elements.sent-2411-130: Utilizing this approach was instrumental in ensuring that our SLR encompassed foundational works and recent advancements within the field of AAD.
elements.sent-2411-131: From here, we create and manually apply a tagging system.
elements.sent-2411-132: The purpose of this step was to be able to systematical filter relevant papers based on what a papers' main focus was.
elements.sent-2411-133: Figure \ref{fig:trends highlights the distribution of applications and digital library used amongst the papers based on our tagging system.}
elements.sent-2411-134: After filtering for relevance and removing duplicates is performed with the first screening.
elements.sent-2411-135: The tagging system enabled us to organize papers systematically, supporting the identification of commonalities and trends in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-136: Pie charts were used to represent distributions across different digital libraries and application areas, facilitating a concise visualization of contributions in each category.

elements.sent-2411-137: The final process of this methodology involved the extraction of all the papers selected for review.
elements.sent-2411-138: To approach this process from a systematic stand point, we constructed a data extraction form for reviewers to extract relevant information from each paper and normalize the process to reduce individual reviewer bias.
elements.sent-2411-139: The extraction process was inspired by Gama et al.~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive , which lays out the basic ingredients for AAD.}
elements.sent-2411-140: The data collection process involved the creation of a standardized data extraction template.
elements.sent-2411-141: This template was structured into several ``blocks" to capture key information from each paper, including the general information, dataset, model, data management, learning, and paper quality blocks.
elements.sent-2411-142: Each block contained specific fields, some with selectable options (e.g., ``application tags'') and others allowing for manual input to capture nuanced information.
elements.sent-2411-143: For example, within the ``learning block'' are specific items for the authors to address including; learning paradigm, learning mode, model adaptation, and ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-144: Each of these items within this block aim to extract specific information from each paper.
elements.sent-2411-145: The final block determines the overall quality of the paper for establishing the potential usage of a paper as a featured paper for final review.
elements.sent-2411-146: This standardized approach minimized individual reviewer bias and ensured consistent data extraction.
elements.sent-2411-147: To ensure only high-quality studies were included, we established a set of threshold criteria.
elements.sent-2411-148: Each paper was evaluated based on its relevance to AAD, credibility of findings, and specific contributions.
elements.sent-2411-149: This rigorous filtering process led to the final selection of 65 high-quality and directly related papers, of which 18 were review papers.
elements.sent-2411-150: An important aspect of this SLR is to examine trends in the selected literature, allowing the data to highlight the primary focus areas and the evolution of AAD for CPS research.
elements.sent-2411-151: This analysis provides a structured view of the research landscape and informs the thematic focus of this SLR.
elements.sent-2411-152: Figure \ref{fig:trends (a) shows the library distribution revealing that the majority of selected papers were sourced from IEEE Xplore (40.0\%), followed by Springer Link (30.8\%) and Science Direct (21.5\%).}
elements.sent-2411-153: This distribution suggests that these databases are central repositories for cybersecurity research, particularly for studies focused on AAD aspects.
elements.sent-2411-154: The prominence of IEEE highlights its significant role in the dissemination of research within technical and engineering domains and aligns well with the focus on adaptive cybersecurity solutions.
elements.sent-2411-155: Figure \ref{fig:trends (b) displays the application distribution across the selected papers, with ICS emerging as the dominant application area, constituting 41.5\% of the studies.}
elements.sent-2411-156: This finding underscores the importance of ICS in AAD since ICS environments often involve complex interactions between physical and digital components.
elements.sent-2411-157: IoT (27.7\%) and smart grid (13.8\%) also represent significant portions of the application distribution, reflecting an increase in cybersecurity challenges within interconnected and critical infrastructure systems.
elements.sent-2411-158: Remaining applications like vehicles (9.2\%) and the power grid (7.7\%), further demonstrate the multidisciplinary nature of AAD research.
elements.sent-2411-159: This demonstrates vulnerabilities across various sectors where adaptive methods are essential to managing dynamic threats.
elements.sent-2411-160: Figure \ref{fig:trends (c) shows the publication trend over the years.}
elements.sent-2411-161: This indicates a noticeable increase in research activity from 2019 onwards and highlights peaks in 2022 and 2023 (each year contributing 18 papers to the final selection).
elements.sent-2411-162: This upward trend reflects a growing recognition of the need for adaptive and cognitive approaches to cybersecurity, especially as cyber threats become more sophisticated and pervasive.
elements.sent-2411-163: The surge in publications over the past five years aligns with advancements in AI and ML, which have enabled the development of more complex and responsive cybersecurity solutions.
elements.sent-2411-164: Anomaly detection methods can be categorized into two categories as offline and online anomaly detection based the nature of data being processed~\cite{Chandola:2009:Anomaly:Detection:Survey, Odiathevar:2019:Hybrid:Offline:Online:IDS .}
elements.sent-2411-165: On the one hand, offline methods are trained from a static dataset.
elements.sent-2411-166: Most offline methods focus on thresholding requiring extensive training~\cite{Ibidunmoye:2017:AAD:Performance:Metric:Streams .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Although offline methods are able to identify complex patterns, they need to be retained whenever model's performance deteriorates.
elements.sent-2411-168: Therefore, as data streams imply rapid contextual changes from a learnt baseline, offline methods are usually prone to produce a significant number of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-169: On the other hand, online methods are usually based on incrementally learning from time windows as new data arrives~\cite{Moriano:2024:Benchmarking:Unsupervised:Online:IDS .}
elements.sent-2411-170: This implies that online methods are better suited to tackle concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-171: AAD based on offline and online methods have been successfully used by researchers to detect cyberattacks in CPS~\cite{Wang:2020:Multi:Agent:Attack:Resilient:Smart:Grid, Gyamfi:2022:Novel:Online:IDS:IoT:OI-SVDD:AS-ELM, Alsulami:2023:Security:Autonomous:Vehicle:Transfer:Learning .}
elements.sent-2411-172: The taxonomy of papers reviewed in this SLR review is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:slr taxonomy .}
elements.sent-2411-173: CPS are vulnerable to cyberattacks such as denial of service (DoS), fuzzing, FDIA, spoofing, and masquerade attacks, among others.
elements.sent-2411-174: In the literature, authors reported other attacks scenarios including advanced persistent threats, jamming, and ransomware.
elements.sent-2411-175: For a comprehensive review of other attacks on CPS, we refer the reader to the work by Yampolskiy et al.~\cite{Yampolskiy:2013:Taxonomy:Attacks:CPS .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Based on the type of CPS, these attacks target different applications.
elements.sent-2411-177: We then consider the domain application to classify existing work.
elements.sent-2411-178: Specifically, we focus on the more prevalent CPS applications including ICS, vehicle, power grid, IoT, and smart grid~\cite{humayed2017cyber .}
elements.sent-2411-179: We categorize existing work based on the type of ML paradigm, namely supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and RL.
elements.sent-2411-180: In supervised learning, models learn from labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-181: In unsupervised learning, models learn and capture the structure of normal/regular data only~\cite{Ahmad:2017:Unsupervised:Real:Time:Anomaly:Detection:Streaming:Data, Munir:2018:DeepAnT:DL:Unsupervised:Anomaly:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Hence, in this work, methods that rely exclusively on benign data during training, also known as one-class classification, are categorized as unsupervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-183: In RL, the goal is to learn from interaction with an environment in order to secure assets.
elements.sent-2411-184: In doing so, RL agents map a state to actions via a policy function to maximize the numerical reward of the signal~\cite{Arshad:2022:Deep:RL:Anomaly:Detection:SLR .}

elements.sent-2411-186: Nakayama et al. \cite{nakayama2019detection proposed granger causality-based kalman filter with adaptive robust thresholding (G-KART) that learns temporal causal relationships between system components, allowing for the detection of stealthy FDIA attacks that traditional methods might miss.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Authors focus on ensuring system security by analyzing interactions between components rather than relying solely on known topological relationships.
elements.sent-2411-188: Each pair of relationships is modeled using kalman filters (KF), which are updated continuously to monitor component states.
elements.sent-2411-189: The adaptive robust thresholding mechanism adjusts the anomaly detection thresholds based on the rolling median of past residuals which makes the system more resilient to noise and gradual data distribution changes.
elements.sent-2411-190: The G-KART model achieved the highest F1 score (0.141) among the tested methods.
elements.sent-2411-191: G-KART also demonstrated a significantly lower false positive rate (0.07) compared to the other models tested.
elements.sent-2411-192: Pan et al. \cite{pan2019threshold proposed a novel threshold-free physical layer authentication (PHY-AUC) method based on ML to enhance the security of wireless industrial CPS in mobile scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Authors discuss that traditional cryptographic methods for message authentication impose high computation burdens, PHY-AUC offers an alternative with lower resource requirements.
elements.sent-2411-194: The previous PHY-AUC methods relay on fixed thresholds however, due to stability issues these iterations are ineffective in mobile environments.
elements.sent-2411-195: The authors presented a solution to this limitation by introducing a ML-based threshold-free PHY-AUC technique that authenticates as a binary classification problem.
elements.sent-2411-196: The method proposed uses channel matices as inputs for supervised ML algorithms to perform the classification of legitimate and illegitimate messages.
elements.sent-2411-197: Authors tested four ML algorithms, the best performing method was the boosted tree ensemble approach.
elements.sent-2411-198: This model contained 128 dimensional channel matrix input and reached an accuracy of 77\% in the multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) scenario.
elements.sent-2411-199: Saez et al. \cite{saez2019context proposed a context-sensitive hybrid modeling framework for cyber-physical manufacturing systems (CPMS) that aims to enhance anomaly detection and diagnosis.}
elements.sent-2411-200: It combines physics-based and data-driven models, using sensor data and expert knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-201: The framework employs context-sensitive thresholds for anomaly detection and classification models for diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-202: Applied to a CNC machine, it improved detection rates from 75\% to 94\%.
elements.sent-2411-203: The system integrates discrete and continuous states, including global operational state definitions.
elements.sent-2411-204: Physics-based models use fundamental equations, while data-driven models predict outputs with historical data.
elements.sent-2411-205: Hybrid modeling estimates variables like current and voltage, enabling accurate fault diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-206: Huang et al.~\cite{huang2020dynamic proposed a dynamic game framework to model long term interactions between stealthy attackers and proactive defenders.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Authors establish the foundation of the work by describing the model being comprised of two players, player 1 (user) and player 2 (defender).
elements.sent-2411-208: The user's type can be either adversarial or legitimate, which creates uncertainty for the defender with the defender's type being based on their level of sophistication (level of security awareness, detection technique, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-209: The game is structured as a multi-stage interaction, where each stage involves a sequential game with incomplete information.
elements.sent-2411-210: The defender uses an IDS to generate alerts but cannot directly identify user types.
elements.sent-2411-211: Bayesian updates refine beliefs about adversarial and legitimate users.
elements.sent-2411-212: The study derives the perfect Bayesian Nash equilibrium (PBNE) to guide strategies.
elements.sent-2411-213: Sophisticated defenders increased payoffs by 56\% and reconnaissance prevention by 41\%.
elements.sent-2411-214: The framework effectively models advanced persistent threats.
elements.sent-2411-215: Mahdavifar et al. \cite{mahdavifar2020dennes introduced the deep embedded neural network expert system (DeNNeS) to address the lack of explainability in DL models that are used for cyberattacks (phishing and malware).}
elements.sent-2411-216: The system extracts refined rules from trained DL networks to enhance explainability and decision-making in cybersecurity applications.
elements.sent-2411-217: The framework consists of foundational elements from the matrix controlled inference engine (MACIE) algorithm (adopted from previous works), which acts as an inference justification.
elements.sent-2411-218: Authors utilized the MACIE inference justification (MIJ) algorithm and an extended version that extracts rules out of a trained multilayer deep neural network (DNN) called deep inference justification (DIJ).
elements.sent-2411-219: DeNNeS achieved a 97.2\% F1 score on a phishing dataset and 91.1\% on a malware dataset.
elements.sent-2411-220: Adaptive moment estimation (ADAM) optimized updates, enhancing classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-221: Quincozes et al. \cite{quincozes2021performance addressed the issue of cybersecurity in CPS by focusing on feature selection to improve intrusion detection across three CPS layers, perception, transmission, and application.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Authors were motivated by CPS integration issues with physical components that face significant security challenges.
elements.sent-2411-223: Authors proposed a method that implements greedy randomized adaptive search procedure (GRASP) with two main phases—construction and local search.
elements.sent-2411-224: GRASP iteratively builds feature subsets and optimizes them to identify the most relevant features for intrusion detection.
elements.sent-2411-225: The GRASP method optimizes both the construction and local search phases to select relevant features across perception, transmission, and application layers.
elements.sent-2411-226: The GRASP method is tested with various classifiers (Random Forest (RF), naive Bayes (NB), J48, etc.), using datasets that are specific to CPS attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-227: Tested with classifiers like RF and J48, GRASP improved F1 scores by 8.29\% over traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-228: RF achieved 99.64\% F1 in the application layer, while J48 scored 98.50\% in the perception layer.
elements.sent-2411-229: The approach enhanced CPS security across multiple layers.
elements.sent-2411-230: Liu et al. \cite{liu2021root introduced a novel data-driven framework for root-cause analysis of anomalies in complex CPS using a spatiotemporal graphical modeling approach based on symbolic dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The framework focuses on discovering and representing causal interactions among subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-232: The authors formulated the root-cause analysis problem as a minimization problem using an inference-based metric and proposed two approximate solutions, sequential state switching (S3) and artificial anomaly association (A3).
elements.sent-2411-233: The effectiveness of these methods was validated using synthetic data and the Tennessee eastman process (TEP) dataset.
elements.sent-2411-234: The S3 method analyzed patterns of subsystem interactions sequentially to identify potential root causes and the A3 method treats root-cause analysis as a classification problem using a neural network (NN) model.
elements.sent-2411-235: Using synthetic and real datasets, the results showed that S3 and A3 could effectively identify faulty nodes, with S3 having a 100\% recall, precision, and F1 score, while A3 achieved a recall of 96.7\%, precision of 90.6\%, and an F1 score of 93.6\%.
elements.sent-2411-236: Althobaiti et al.~\cite{althobaiti2021intelligent introduced a novel cognitive computing-based IDS for industrial CPS that leverages AI to address security issues.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The approach uses a binary bacterial foraging optimization (BBFO) for feature selection and a gated recurrent unit (GRU) for classification.
elements.sent-2411-238: BBFO is inspired by the foraging behavior of bacteria, where bacteria move towards optimal solutions in the search space.
elements.sent-2411-239: This approach aims to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset, improving computational efficiency and model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-240: Nesterov-accelerated adaptive moment estimation (NADAM) is employed to optimize the hyperparameters of the GRU.
elements.sent-2411-241: Applied to industrial datasets, the system achieved 98.45\% detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-242: The method combines high precision with reduced computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-243: Vavra et al.~\cite{vavra2021adaptive , presented a comprehensive approach to anomaly detection in ICS using ML algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Recognizing the critical role of ICS in modern society and the increasing risks posed by cyberattacks, the authors develop an AAD system designed to address key challenges, including the detection of unknown attacks, scalability, adaptability, high false alarm rates, and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-245: The system integrates artificial neural networks (ANN), long short-term memory networks (LSTM), isolation forest (IF), and one-class support vector machines (OCSVM), each adapted for semi-supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-246: This approach enables the system to identify anomalies by learning from normal operational data and flagging deviations as potential cyberattacks.
elements.sent-2411-247: The use of semi-supervised learning is particularly important for detecting unknown attacks that may not be present in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-248: The paper also emphasizes the importance of data preprocessing and feature selection, noting that issues like missing values, feature scaling, and high dimensionality of data can significantly impact the performance of ML models.
elements.sent-2411-249: Techniques such as principal component analysis (PCA) are employed to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset while retaining the most critical information, thereby enhancing the system's efficiency and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-250: The paper concludes that the proposed system effectively balances detection accuracy with computational efficiency, particularly when the IF algorithm is optimized using genetic algorithms (GA).
elements.sent-2411-251: The system balances detection accuracy and computational demands, achieving strong performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-252: Alohali et al. \cite{alohali2022artificial proposed an AI-enabled multimodal fusion-based intrusion detection system (AIMMF-IDS) that employs an improved fish swarm optimization (IFSO-FS) technique for feature selection by using the Levy Flight concept to enhance searching capability and avoid local optima problems.}
elements.sent-2411-253: The system integrates recurrent neural network (RNN), bi-directional LSTM (Bi-LSTM), and DBN models for multimodal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-254: Applied to NSL-KDD and CICIDS datasets, it achieved precision, recall, and F1 scores above 95\%.
elements.sent-2411-255: The model addresses intrusion detection challenges with robust performance.
elements.sent-2411-256: Ibor et al. \cite{ibor2022novel presented a novel hybrid approach to predict cyberattacks on CPS communication networks.}
elements.sent-2411-257: The proposed method leverages a bio-inspired hyperparameter search technique to improve the structure of a deep neural network (DNN).
elements.sent-2411-258: The GA operates by generating an initial population of neural network (NN) structures (chromosomes), evaluating their performance using a fitness function, and then applying selection, recombination, and mutation to generate improved structures over multiple generations.
elements.sent-2411-259: Tested on CICIDS2017 and UNSW-NB15 datasets, the method achieved 99.81\% and 80.10\% accuracy, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-260: The approach optimizes neural network architecture for better attack prediction with minimal false positives.
elements.sent-2411-261: Liu et al. \cite{liu2022intrusion investigated enhancing cybersecurity for maritime transportation systems (MTS) using IoT technology.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Authors proposed a federated learning-based IDS called ``FedBatch" that employs a hybrid convolutional neural network (CNN) multilayer perceptron (CNN-MLP) model.
elements.sent-2411-263: The model addresses the issue of maintaining data privacy and handling the straggler problem (delays in data/model updates due to unstable communication in maritime settings).
elements.sent-2411-264: It adapts to unstable communication environments using dynamic aggregation methods.
elements.sent-2411-265: Tested on independent and identically distributed (IID) and non-IID datasets, FedBatch achieved 88.1\% accuracy, outperforming traditional federated learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-266: Kure et al.~\cite{kure2022asset presented a unified approach to cybersecurity risk management (CSRM) for CPS by integrating fuzzy set theory and ML classifiers.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The focus is on predicting risk types, assessing asset criticality, and evaluating the effectiveness of existing controls.
elements.sent-2411-268: Fuzzy set theory is applied to assess the criticality of CPS assets, considering security factors such as confidentiality, integrity, availability, accountability, and conformance.
elements.sent-2411-269: Multiple ML classifiers, including k-nearest neighbors (k-NN), decision tree (DT), RF, and NB, are used to predict different risk types like DoS, cyber espionage, and crimeware.
elements.sent-2411-270: DT achieved 93\% accuracy in predicting risk types.
elements.sent-2411-271: Fuzzy logic improves asset assessment, supporting proactive risk management.
elements.sent-2411-272: Shi et al. \cite{shi2023lstm proposed a ML-driven online side-channel monitoring approach that utilizes an LSTM autoencoder (AE) for detecting unintended alterations in the additive manufacturing (AM) process.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Authors were motivated by the growing vulnerabilities in AM due to potential cyber-physical attacks.
elements.sent-2411-274: Traditional monitoring methods fail to detect internal alterations in AM parts, which can severely compromise the functionality and mechanical properties of the product.
elements.sent-2411-275: Both supervised and unsupervised monitoring schemes were implemented, with experimental validation conducted on a fused filament fabrication (FFF) platform equipped with accelerometers.
elements.sent-2411-276: The AE, consisting of an encoder and a decoder, where the encoder reduces high-dimensional input data into a compact latent representation using an encoding function implemented with LSTM layers, which effectively capture temporal dependencies in sequential sensor data.
elements.sent-2411-277: The decoder reconstructs the original input from the latent representation using a decoding function.
elements.sent-2411-278: The reconstruction error quantifies the difference between the original input and the reconstructed data, serving as a measure of how well the model represents the input.
elements.sent-2411-279: Two cases are considered for this investigation, Case 1 involves inserting a void into the design geometry at the STL stage, while Case 2 alters layer thickness at the slicing stage.
elements.sent-2411-280: In supervised monitoring, the proposed method achieved high F1 scores of 0.95 in Case 1 and 0.96 in Case 2, outperforming traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-281: In unsupervised monitoring, the LSTM-AE with OCSVM exponentially weighted moving average (OCSVM-EWMA) demonstrated a low false alarm rate (0.09 in Case 1 and 0.03 in Case 2) and fast attack response times (4.6 and 7.4 samples, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-282: Intriago et al. \cite{intriago2023real introduced a Hoeffding adaptive tree (HAT) classifier combined with an instance selection algorithm to detect cyber and non-cyber contingencies in real-time for cyber-physical power systems (CPPS).}
elements.sent-2411-283: Authors are motivated based on challenges presented in continuous monitoring of CPPS through wide-area monitoring, protection, and control (WAMPAC) systems that handle high-velocity and unbounded data streams from devices like phasor measurement units (PMUs).
elements.sent-2411-284: Authors addressed this issue by creating a streaming learning classification system that adapts to evolving data streams.
elements.sent-2411-285: They proposed a three-stage instance selection process involving reordering, resetting outdated data, and dynamic window size selection.
elements.sent-2411-286: The classifier outperformed existing methods in six case studies, achieving over 99\% accuracy on multiclass datasets.
elements.sent-2411-287: The HAT stream instance selection (HAT+SIS) classifier achieved over 99\% accuracy in the multiclass dataset, maintained this accuracy even as the data evolved, and outperformed HAT drift detection method (HAT+DDM) and Hoeffding tree (HT+DDM).
elements.sent-2411-288: These methods experienced significant accuracy drops around 4,000 instances.
elements.sent-2411-289: HAT+SIS also processed approximately 5,000 instances in 43 seconds and maintained a stable model size of around 196 KB, demonstrating both efficiency and scalability for real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-290: Alshammari~\cite{alshammari2023design proposed a Bayesian neural network (BNN) architecture for evaluating the statistical features of DoS attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-291: In the investigation, the NSL-KDD dataset is used which is a comprehensive network security dataset.
elements.sent-2411-292: The paper emphasizes selecting features with the highest predictive power while minimizing redundancy which is established with a heatmap of feature mean values.
elements.sent-2411-293: The BNN applies the local markov property to forecast event probabilities and represent probabilistic relationships between different variables using a directed acyclic graph.
elements.sent-2411-294: The BNN is constructed with multiple nodes, each of the nodes represents a random variable with the edges showing conditional dependencies between variables.
elements.sent-2411-295: The preprocessed data is trained using the BNN to learn patterns and relationships in the network traffic.
elements.sent-2411-296: The author states that the BNN demonstrated a testing accuracy of 97.5 \% with other metrics highlighting that the BNN was capable of generalizing well.
elements.sent-2411-297: When compared to accuracies from DT (78\%-89\%) and ANN (87\%-89\%), the BNN outperforms these traditional networks in terms of identifying anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-298: Wang et al. \cite{wang2024real introduced a novel integrated ML and DL approach for real-time attack detection and identification.}
elements.sent-2411-299: The authors present a two-stage solution involving a OCSVM for detecting whether a CPS is under attack, followed by a pairwise self-supervised long short-term memory (PSLSTM) model for identifying the attack type.
elements.sent-2411-300: The method is designed to detect known attack types and discover unknown new attacks.
elements.sent-2411-301: The two-stage learning approach begins with the OCSVM model that is trained exclusively on data representing the normal state of the CPS.
elements.sent-2411-302: The OCSVM model functions by defining a hyperplane that separates normal data points from potential outliers.
elements.sent-2411-303: The second stage begins once the attack is detected.
elements.sent-2411-304: PSLSTM is used to identify the specific attack type and consists of several LSTM networks that effectively convert a multi-class problem into a binary classification task.
elements.sent-2411-305: The number of pairwise models to be trained is calculated from a given \(\mathbf{K}\) known attack types and \(\frac{\mathbf{K (\mathbf{K -1) {2}\) LSTM models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: The OCSVM-PSLSTM method demonstrated high-quality performance with real-time attack detection and identification by achieving an average accuracy of 99.7\% for identifying known attack types and 97.2\% with discovering unknown attacks.
elements.sent-2411-307: Mitchell et al. \cite{mitchell2013survivability developed a mathematical model to assess the survivability of mobile CPS (MCPS) using dynamic voting-based intrusion detection.}
elements.sent-2411-308: They employed a stochastic Petri net (SPN) model to analyze trade-offs between energy conservation and intrusion tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-309: The system dynamically adapts to changing system states and environmental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-310: As the proportion of compromised nodes or energy levels change, the model adjusts the intrusion detection interval \(T_{IDS}\) and the number of detectors in real time to maintain optimal system performance.
elements.sent-2411-311: The dynamic voting-based technique continuously updates its parameters without requiring pre-labeled data, allowing the system to respond to attacks and energy constraints as they occur.
elements.sent-2411-312: The results demonstrate that there is an optimal \(T_{IDS}\) that maximizes the mean time to failure (MTTF) of the MCPS by balancing energy consumption and intrusion tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-313: Authors found that an optimal value of \(T_{IDS}\)=160s with 5 detectors provided the highest MTTF for their reference system.
elements.sent-2411-314: The model's predictions were validated through simulations, showing a close match with theoretical results, with only a 4.60\% to 7.64\% mean percentage error.
elements.sent-2411-315: Meria et al. \cite{meira2020performance examined six unsupervised algorithms; AE, one-class nearest neighbor, IF, one-class $\mathcal{K}$-means, one-class scaled convex hull, and OCSVM, on two public datasets (NSL-KDD and ISCX) for anomaly detection in cybersecurity.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Data was preprocessed by normalization using $\mathcal{Z}$-score and Min Max.
elements.sent-2411-317: The one-class scaled convex hull achieved the highest AUC on the NSL-KDD dataset with an AUC value of 85.30\%.
elements.sent-2411-318: The one-class nearest neighbor performed best on the ISCX dataset with an AUC of 95.20\%.
elements.sent-2411-319: Overall, the one-class nearest neighbor, one-class scaled convex hull, and OCSVM demonstrated the best performance across both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-320: Xi et al.~\cite{xi2023adaptive proposed the adaptive-correlation-aware unsupervised DL (ACUDL) which addresses the challenge of detecting anomalies in high-dimensional, noisy, and unlabeled data.}
elements.sent-2411-321: The core innovation of ACUDL is its use of a dynamic graph structure to represent and update the implicit correlations among data points, which is critical for accurately capturing the underlying relationships in CPS data.
elements.sent-2411-322: ACUDL begins by constructing an initial directed graph using the KNN algorithm, where each node in the graph represents a data point, and edges represent the correlations between these points.
elements.sent-2411-323: This graph is then dynamically updated during training to reflect changes in the data, using an adaptive mechanism that adjusts the graph structure based on the training loss.
elements.sent-2411-324: The model also incorporates a dual-AE (D-AE) framework that separately encodes the original non-correlation features, the correlation features extracted from the graph, and a decoder.
elements.sent-2411-325: These features are then fused and passed through a gaussian mixture model (GMM) to estimate the anomaly energy, which is used to detect anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-326: Through extensive experiments on various CPS datasets, including scenarios like smart healthcare systems (SHS) and intelligent cruise control systems (ICCS), the authors demonstrate that ACUDL significantly outperforms existing DL-based anomaly detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-327: Experiments on CPS datasets demonstrated its superiority, achieving AUC scores of 73.3\% and 87.5\%, with strong F1 scores and precision.
elements.sent-2411-328: ACUDL effectively handles high-dimensional, noisy data.
elements.sent-2411-329: Cai et al. proposed an adaptive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) mitigation scheme for software-defined networking (SDN)~\cite{cai2023adam .}
elements.sent-2411-330: Authors proposed the adaptive DDoS attack mitigation (ADAM) scheme that combines information entropy and unsupervised anomaly detection methods to detect both known and unknown DDoS attacks in software-defined CPS (SD-CPS).
elements.sent-2411-331: Motivated by the increasing vulnerability of CPS to DDoS attacks due to insecure or outdated components, the authors highlight the limitations of traditional defense mechanisms that rely on static thresholds or predefined attack signatures.
elements.sent-2411-332: ADAM operates through three stages: nominal, detection, and mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-333: In the nominal stage, a "nominal profile" of normal network traffic is created by sampling traffic and calculating entropy vectors for features such as IP addresses and port numbers.
elements.sent-2411-334: ADAM achieves a high accuracy of 99.13\% on average in mitigating various DDoS attacks, with a significantly reduced false-positive rate compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-335: This suggests that ADAM is a scalable and effective solution for SD-CPS environments.

elements.sent-2411-348: Feng et al.~\cite{Feng:2020:Efficient:Drone:Hijacking:XGBoost proposed an efficient drone hijacking detection method that consumes inertial measurement unit (IMU) (i.e., gyroscope and accelerometer) and GPS (i.e., longitude and latitude) data.}
elements.sent-2411-349: The proposed method used the eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) algorithm to mine the relationship between IMU and GPS data using real-time data samples to decide if the drone has been hijacked or not.

elements.sent-2411-353: Prediction correctness in each sample time was reported as $96.3 \%$ and $100\%$ in hijack and normal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-354: As the proposed model is deployed in online fashion it can achieve $100\%$ detection correctness just after 1 second after the hijack starts.
elements.sent-2411-355: Alsulami et al.~\cite{Alsulami:2023:Security:Autonomous:Vehicle:Transfer:Learning proposed an intelligent intrusion detection systems (IIDS) for autonomous vehicle-cyber physical systems (AV-CPS) that focuses on transfer learning.}
elements.sent-2411-356: Specifically, the proposed method used eight pre-trained CNNs, including InceptionV3, ResNet-50, ShuffleNet, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, ResNet-18, SqueezeNet, and AlexNet.
elements.sent-2411-357: By leveraging pre-trained models to enhance the performance of IIDS, authors target the detection of anomalous communications in the controller area network (CAN) bus having an effect on the connected physical components of AVs.
elements.sent-2411-358: Authors' simulation setup include a self-driven car system consisting of a lead and an ego vehicle (self-driving car).
elements.sent-2411-359: In an ideal situation, the ego vehicle maintains its distance from the lead vehicle using the adaptive cruise control system.
elements.sent-2411-360: Authors simulate a CAN communication network environment using Simulink and produced a dataset consisting of (1) position of the ego vehicle, (2) velocity of the ego vehicle, (3) position of the lead vehicle, and (4) velocity of the lead vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-361: Note that for the data be processed by the CNNs, time series representations of captured signals are tranformed into a 2-dimentional representation (i.e., images).
elements.sent-2411-362: They found that GoogLeNet performed best achieving $99.47\%$ on F1 score metric.
elements.sent-2411-363: Mowlka et al.~\cite{Mowla:2020:AFRL:Adaptive:Federated:RL:Jamming proposed an adaptive federated reinforcement learning-based jamming attack defense strategy to protect flying ad-hoc networks (FANETs), i.e., a decentralized communication network unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).}
elements.sent-2411-364: They focused on a model-free Q-learning mechanism with adaptive exploration-exploitation epsilon greedy policy, directed by an on-device federated jamming detection mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-365: Q-learning learns a policy maximizing total rewards based on trial-and-error.
elements.sent-2411-366: That is, a UAV client receives a negative reward if it is moved closer to the jammer location.
elements.sent-2411-367: Here, an epsilon-greedy policy is used to balance the outcome of exploration and exploitation opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-368: They showed that the proposed adaptive federated RL-based approach performed better spatial retreat defense strategies.
elements.sent-2411-369: Table~\ref{table: Adaptive anomaly detection in vehicular applications summarizes key attributes of the papers reviewed in Section~\ref{subsec:Vehicle .}}

elements.sent-2411-370: Cui et al.~\cite{Cui:2021:Source:Authentication:Distributed:Synchrophasors:Microgrids proposed a hybrid approach combining self-adaptive mathematical morphology (SAMM) and time frequency (TF) techniques to authenticate source information on distribution synchrophasors (DS) within microgrids at near-range locations.}
elements.sent-2411-371: Their proposed method is intended to deter ``source ID mix'' data spoofing attacks on DS as they threat to the security of the power grid.
elements.sent-2411-372: This attack can manipulate source information of DS without changing the measurement values.
elements.sent-2411-373: Manipulating the source information on DS has an effect on critical synchrophasor-based control and applications including wide-area damping and control and disturbance localization.
elements.sent-2411-374: SAMM allows for adaptive regulation of synchrophasors variations which represent local environmental characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-375: In addition, TF mapping is used to extract frequency-related features from the regulated synchrophasors variation.
elements.sent-2411-376: They used a RF classifier to correlate the extracted signatures with the source information based on the TF analysis.
elements.sent-2411-377: Their proposed approach consists of three steps including (1) a high-pass filter to extract frequency variation on the original DS data; (2) an integrated SAMM-TF used to extract useful features from the synchrophasors variation; and (3) the integration with a random forest classifier for source integration.
elements.sent-2411-378: The SAMM approach adaptively preserves significant peaks in frequency so that distinctive signatures can be extracted for source authentication.
elements.sent-2411-379: They validated their results using distribution synchrophasors from multiple small geographical scales validating the proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-380: Khan et al.~\cite{Khan:2021:Privacy:Preserving:IDS:Power:Networks proposed a privacy-conserving based intrusion detection (PC-IDS) for contemporary smart power systems (SPNs) using a hybrid ML approach.}
elements.sent-2411-381: The proposed framework consist of two main components: data pre-processing and intrusion detection.
elements.sent-2411-382: Data pre-processing entails attribute/feature mapping, reduction, and normalization.
elements.sent-2411-383: This allows to process diverse types of attributes such as numerical and categorical features.
elements.sent-2411-384: The intrusion detection module comprises stages of training and detection.
elements.sent-2411-385: Specifically, it uses a PNN and consumes several types of regular and malicious patterns to enhance classification performance.
elements.sent-2411-386: Particle swarm optimization (PSO) is used to select the hyperparameters of the PNN model.
elements.sent-2411-387: The performance of the proposed framework is evaluated in two commonly available datasets: power system~\cite{Morris:2013:ICS:Attack:Datasets and UNSW-NB15~\cite{Moustafa:2015:UNSW:Dataset datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-388: Their experimental evaluation shows the effectiveness of the proposed framework to protect data from SPNs and determine anomalous behavior in terms of traditional evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-389: Jiao et al.~\cite{Jiao:2022:Cyberattack:Resilient:Forecasting:Adaptive:Robust:Regression proposed a cyberattack-resilient load forecasting approach based on robust regression, i.e., adaptive least trimmed squares (ALTS)~\cite{Bacher:2016:Adaptive:Robust:Regression:ALTS .}}
elements.sent-2411-390: Authors assume that adversaries alter load entries in the training data so that the estimated regression coefficients become inaccurate resulting in forecasts that may lead to poor decision making.
elements.sent-2411-391: They used two different attack types: random and ramping attacks.
elements.sent-2411-392: In random attacks, a randomly selected proportion of the training data is scaled by a random factor following, for example, a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-393: In ramping attacks, many single attack intervals are injected parametrized by starting attack point and length.
elements.sent-2411-394: They injected these attacks to alter the GEFCom2012 dataset~\cite{Hong:2014:Global:Enery:Forecasting:Dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-395: Their robust approach focused on estimating robust estimators for the coefficients in the regression models based on M-estimation~\cite{Huber:1992:Robust:Estimation:Location:Parameter ---a generalization of the maximum likelihood estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-396: The M-estimator is obtained through the iterative re-weighted least squares (IRLS) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-397: The core of their adaptive algorithm is based on least trimmed squares (LTS) which is a robust alternative to ordinary LS when dealing with linear regression problems.
elements.sent-2411-398: LTS minimizes the sum of square residuals over a subset of the whole datapoints by excluding a proportion of $p$ data points whose residuals are the largest in magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-399: Thus $p$ is a tuning parameter that leads to the ALTS method.
elements.sent-2411-400: This provides robustness to potential outliers.
elements.sent-2411-401: They compute goodness of fit using mean absolute percentage error.
elements.sent-2411-402: Five methods including, LS, M-Huber, M-bisquare, L$_{1}$, and ALTS ere used to fit the model and perform forecasting in the validation dataset.
elements.sent-2411-403: A comparison analysis using the GEFCom2012 dataset suggests that the ALTS method is robust against to random and ramping attacks both when the proportion of attack data is high and the robustness does not decrease as attack data proportion increases.
elements.sent-2411-404: Ding et al.~\cite{Ding:2022:Data:Driven:Situational:Awarness:Power:systems proposed a data-driven security situational awareness framework to secure power systems.}
elements.sent-2411-405: Their proposed framework focused on an adaptive honeypot architecture for capturing system logs and network traffic in distributed fashion.
elements.sent-2411-406: Specifically, they deployed 10 honeypots around the world by simulating industrial control devices in power systems to lure attackers.
elements.sent-2411-407: Their deployed honeypot architecture includes multiple industrial protocols including Modbus, Siemens S7-Comm, Guardian$\_$ast, Kamstrup$\_$382, Bacnet, Http and Ipmi.
elements.sent-2411-408: The honeypot architecture was deployed using the Alibaba cloud environment.
elements.sent-2411-409: Security incidents are detected by modeling the captured attack traffic from honeypots and the security scanning system.
elements.sent-2411-410: In doing so, they constructed a security situation graph based on traffic logs by modeling IP instances as nodes in a graph.
elements.sent-2411-411: They annotate the nodes in the graph using word2vec~\cite{Mikolov:2013:Efficient:Estimation:Word:Representations:Word2Vec by processing attributed information of IPs.}
elements.sent-2411-412: Their detection algorithms leverage a graph convolutional network (GCN) to detect malicious IPs.
elements.sent-2411-413: Under this approach, the GCN learns the features of malicious IPs in a supervised fashion.
elements.sent-2411-414: They performed experiments to evaluate performance of TCP SYN probe in their deployed scanning system.
elements.sent-2411-415: They tested two attack configurations: single port scanning and multi-port scanning.
