elements.subsection-2411-006: AAD in the Internet of Things (IoT)
elements.sent-2411-417: Li et al.~\cite{Li:2019:System:Statistics:Learning:IoT introduced a statistical learning based anomaly detection technique that monitors the operation of IoT devices to detect possible cyberattacks and malicious activities.}
elements.sent-2411-418: In particular, they used time series derived from system statistics including CPU usage, memory consumption, and network throughput, among others, to model normal behavior.
elements.sent-2411-419: To do so, they simulated a real IoT system operation made of 12 beagle bone black (BBB) connected to a router in a LAN topology.
elements.sent-2411-420: In particular, they ran a program that samples random data with a fixed interval and processes a variety of signal processing, storage, compression, and transmitting operations.
elements.sent-2411-421: They injected simulated cyberattacks including unauthorized access, port scan, virus, and flood.
elements.sent-2411-422: They trained LR, NN, and recurrent neural network (RNN) classifiers to predict normal system behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-423: These models were trained using different window sizes showing that there is a threshold for which the MAE starts getting diminishing returns.
elements.sent-2411-424: Their results showed that the NN perform the best at the expense of higher computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-425: The thresholding for deciding if a particular data sample is an anomaly or not is performed through local outlier factor (LOF), cumulative statistics thresholding (CUSUM), and adaptive online thresholding (AOT).
elements.sent-2411-426: They showed that the method using AOT perform better than LOF and CUSUM based on F1 score evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-427: Gopalakrishnan et al.~\cite{Gopalakrishnan:2020:DL:Data:Offloading:Mobile:Edge:Computing:System introduced a deep learning based traffic prediction framework with offloading mechanism and cyberatack detection (DLTPDO-CD).}
elements.sent-2411-428: Their proposed approach is composed of three major processes involving traffic prediction, data offloading, and attack detection.
elements.sent-2411-429: In doing so, it includes first a BiLSTM based traffic prediction process to enable proficient data offloading.
elements.sent-2411-430: Considering a double LSTM is meant to enhance learning long-term dependencies as it enhances the accuracy of the detection.
elements.sent-2411-431: Second, an adaptive sampling cross entropy technique (ASCE) is incorporated to maximize network throughput by deciding offloading users from the network.
elements.sent-2411-432: Lastly, for the detection of cyberattacks in mobile edge computing, they used a DBN optimized using a barnacles mating optimizer (BMO).
elements.sent-2411-433: Their approach was tested on simulated data showing better performance over compared methods under different dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-434: Bibi et al.~\cite{Bibi:2022:Deep:AI:Cyber:Threat:Analysis:IIoT proposed an efficient and self-learning autonomous multivector threat intelligence and detection mechanism to proactively defend IIoT networks.}
elements.sent-2411-435: Their approach used a convolutional LSTM2D (Cu-ConvLSTM2D mechanism) being highly scalable with self-optimized capabilities to detect diverse and dynamic variant of IIoT threats.
elements.sent-2411-436: Cu-ConvLSTM2D is a recurrent layer similar to LSTM, but the internal matrix multiplication is exchanged with convolution operations.
elements.sent-2411-437: They evaluated their proposed framework on a Kitsune surveillance network intrusion dataset~\cite{Mirsky:2018:Kitsune comprising 21 million instances of varying attack patterns and prevalent threat vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-438: The proposed technique outperforms current contemporary DL-driven architectures and existing benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-439: Yazdinejad et al.~\cite{Yazdinejad:2023:Secure:Fuzzy:Blockchain:Framework proposed a novel design and implementation of a secure and intelligent fuzzy blockchain framework.}
elements.sent-2411-440: Their proposed framework focus on three layers: IoT, blockchain, and intelligent fuzzy layers.
elements.sent-2411-441: The IoT layer hanldes smart devices that communicate with each other in the blockchain environment.
elements.sent-2411-442: The blockchain layer handles IoT device management ensuring safe channels for the transmission of data and transactions between IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-443: In their intelligent fuzzy layer, they used a threat detection in the blockchain layer.
elements.sent-2411-444: Their threat detection module is based on a fuzzy DL model that uses a fully connected network based on fuzzy neurons to output the aggregation of classification results of several classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-445: In conjuction, an adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS) model is used to design an optimal fuzzy system for threat detection in IoT networks.
elements.sent-2411-446: The ANFIS model estimates input membership functions and output modified membership functions.
elements.sent-2411-447: Finally, a fuzzy control system module leverages previous inputs in both blockchain and IoT layers to feed a fuzzy control system module to arrive at complex decision making.
elements.sent-2411-448: Their proposed framework was tested for threat detection in the Ethereum blockchain~\cite{Jung:2019:Data:Mining:Ethereum:Fraud:Detection, Al:2020:Labeled:Transactions:Dataset:Ethereum and the NSL-KDD~\cite{Bala:2019:KDD:Cup:99:Dataset datasets for blockchain-enabled IoT networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-449: They verified the efficiency in both blockchain and IoT network sides using a variety of evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-450: Dey~\cite{Dey:2023:Hybrid:Meta:Heuristic:Feature:Selection:Attack:Detection proposed a hybrid feature selection scheme combining statistical filter approaches including $\chi^2$, Pearson's correlation coefficient, and mutual information combined with a non-dominant sorting GA (NSGA-II) metaheuristic for optimizing feature selection.}
elements.sent-2411-451: Specifically, filter selection methods are used to rank features based on their importance and subsequent initialization of NSGA-II allowing faster convergence to the solution.
elements.sent-2411-452: NSGA-II belongs to the evolutionary algorithms class focusing on selection, crossover, and mutation steps.
elements.sent-2411-453: Resulting populations are sorted from top to bottom without losing reasonable solutions.
elements.sent-2411-454: After selecting the most relevant features for the classification task, a SVM classifier is used in the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-455: The proposed framework is tested from a publicly available network traffic dataset (ToN-IoT) collected at a large-scale and realistic network from the Cyber Range and IoT Labs at the School of Engineering and Information Technology at UNSW Camberra~\cite{Booij:2021:TON:IoT:Dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-456: ToN-IoT covers a variety of cyberattacks inclusing ransomware, DoS, and DDoS.
elements.sent-2411-457: The proposed method reached optimal performance (99.48$\%$ accuracy) with only 13 features.
elements.sent-2411-458: Yazdinejad et al.~\cite{Yazdinejad:2023:Ensemble:DL:Threat:Hunting:IoT proposed an ensemble DL model that combines LSTM and AEs to detect anomalous activities in IIoT.}
elements.sent-2411-459: Output data is classified as normal or abnormal via a DT after inspecting the reconstruction error between the input and the output layer of the LSTM AE.
elements.sent-2411-460: The proposed model is evaluated in two real IoT datasets, i.e., gas pipeline and secure water treatment, which are imbalanced and have temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-461: The proposed framework outperforms conventional classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-462: Despite being a promising approach for detecting anomalies in time series data, the use of LSTM requires significantly higher training than simpler models.
elements.sent-2411-463: Jullian et al.~\cite{Jullian:2023:DL:Cyberattacks:IoT:Networks:Distributed implemented a distributed framework based on DL to detect different source of vulnerabilities in the IoT.}
elements.sent-2411-464: Their approach consists of mainly four stages: data treatment and preprocessing, DL model training and testing, distributed framework deployment, and attack detection and classification.
elements.sent-2411-465: Their proposed approach was tested on two different datasets: the BoT-IoT dataset addresing specific attacks of IoT environments and the NSL-KDD dataset to broaden the types of cyberattacks.
elements.sent-2411-466: Due to huge imbalances in the proportion of attack vs. benign samples, they applied undersampling based on the large number of records.
elements.sent-2411-467: A standard normalization procedure is applied to both datasets to prevent models overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-468: Once the datasets are preprocessed, they evaluated two different models: a feed forward NN (FFNN) and a LSTM.
elements.sent-2411-469: Their proposed approach used a federated learning architecture to train models in a distributed fashion relying on the communication between fog and central servers.
elements.sent-2411-470: To prevent overfitting during the training procedure, they ran an optimization procedure to select the best combination of hyperparameters to achieve best evaluation metrics inclusing accuracy, precision, and recall.
elements.sent-2411-471: Their proposed distributed framework was found effective in different types of attacks achieving an accuracy of up to $99.95 \%$ across the different setups.
elements.sent-2411-472: Basati and Faghih~\cite{Basati:2023:APAE:IoT:IDS:Symmetric:Parallel:Autoecoder introduced an intelligent IDS framework based on an asymmetric parallel AE able to detect various attacks in IoT networks.}
elements.sent-2411-473: In their preprocessing stage, they transformed 1D traffic feature vectors into 2D feature vectors with equal width and height.
elements.sent-2411-474: To extract features from more distant neighbors and association among long-range features they integrate dilatated convolution~\cite{Yu:2015:Multi:Scale:Dilatated:Convolutions with self-attention~\cite{Vaswani:2017:Attention:is:all:You:Need .}}
elements.sent-2411-475: Their approach called APAE contains two asymmetric AEs in parallel, each of them containing three successive layers of convolutional filters.
elements.sent-2411-476: APAE was evaluated in three popular public datasets named UNSW-NB15, CICIDS2017, and KDDCup99.
elements.sent-2411-477: Results showed that the proposed framework offers superior results than the state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-478: Gupta et al.~\cite{Gupta:2023:Integration:Digital:Twin:FL:VIoT developed a hierarchical federated learning (HFL) anomaly detection approach to address security and data privacy concerns in the context of vehicular IoT (V-IoT).}
elements.sent-2411-479: By creating a digital twin of an intelligent transportation environment, they leveraged a comprehensive virtual replica for detecting malicious activities using an anomaly detection model.
elements.sent-2411-480: To further expand the capabilities of federated learning (FL) for multi use scenarios, they developed a FL approach that allows the aggregation of gradients at multiple levels enabling the participation of multiple entities.
elements.sent-2411-481: Their proposed framework has six phases: (1) initial phase, where smart vehicle data collection begins; (2) functional phase, where supplementary data from the external environment provide context; (3) analytic phase, where a digital twin is created for each vehicle in the V-IoT and data mining algorithms are applied to previously collected data; (4) identifying anomaly phase, where anomaly detection algorithms based on LSTM are trained to distinguish between normal and abnormal patterns; (5) collaborative phase, where the output of multiple anomaly detection algorithms is processed to improve evaluation metrics; and (6) reporting and decision phase, where anomalies are reported to relevant stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-482: Yasaey et al.~\cite{Yasaei:2020:IoT:CAD:Context:Aware:Anomaly:Detection:Through:Sensor:Association proposed an adaptive context-aware anomaly detection method for securing IoT sensor data intended to run on a fog computing platform.}
elements.sent-2411-483: Their approach is based on a sensor association algorithm that generates fingerprint of sensors and then cluster them to extract the context of the system.
elements.sent-2411-484: Context generation is completed by extracting the binary fingerprinting sensor values.
elements.sent-2411-485: As sensors that are affected by an event are expected to have similar patterns in their fingerprints, a clustering algorithm based on minimizing the distance between binary codifications and the Hamming distance is used.
elements.sent-2411-486: By relying on contextual information, they used a LSTM neural network and a Gaussian consensus estimator to identify the source of anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-487: In particular, for each cluster of sensors, a LSTM neural network produces a set of predictive models for each cluster.
elements.sent-2411-488: A multivariate Gaussian estimator is used to help modeling whether reconstruction errors between the real and predicted values match with the system's normal behavior.
elements.sent-2411-489: To infer the source of the anomaly a consensus algorithms checks the consistency of sensor behaviors within clusters and across clusters.
elements.sent-2411-490: Finally, to adapt the inference models with respect to concept drift, their proposed approach allows two levels of update: complete (where all the modules are retrained in order) and partial (which only retrains the predictor model).
elements.sent-2411-491: The adaptation decision is triggered based on changes in the distribution of the data.
elements.sent-2411-492: They tested their approach on the environmental training center waste water plant in Riccione~\cite{Giannoni:2018:Anomaly:Detection:IoT:Data with synthetic anomalies.}
elements.sent-2411-493: Gyamfi and Jarcut et al.~\cite{Gyamfi:2022:Novel:Online:IDS:IoT:OI-SVDD:AS-ELM proposed a lightweight network intrusion detection system (NIDS) to secure industrial IoT (I-IoT) relying on an online incremental support vector data description (OI-SVDD) anomaly detection on the IIoT devices and an adaptive sequential extreme learning machine (AS-ELM) on a multiaccess edge computing (MEC) server.}
elements.sent-2411-494: In their design, the OI-SVDD model is placed on the IIoT device and the AS-ELM model is placed on the MEC server at the edge network to perform deep network attack duties.
elements.sent-2411-495: Their OI-SVDD is based on using an incremental learning scheme to add samples to the training function process consisting only of support vectors at each stage.
elements.sent-2411-496: During AS-ELM, a training dataset is utilized during the initialization phase and then testing data is processed chunk by chunk.
elements.sent-2411-497: Authors tested their proposed NIDS on the UNSW-NB15 dataset and a self generated dataset showing effective performance for detecting intrusions in a realistic IIoT environment.
elements.sent-2411-498: Li et al.~\cite{Li:2022:Few:Shot:IoT:Attack:Detection:Unsupervised:Domain:Adaptive:Regularization proposed an adversarial unsupervised domain-adaptive regularization based on a recursive feature pyramid CNN (RFP-CNN) to detect IoT attacks more effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-499: As a first step, they developed a recursive feature pyramid network (FPN) architecture~\cite{Arrington:2016:Behavioral:Modeling:IDS:Via:Immunity:Inspired:Algorithms to extract high-level features of the RFP-CNN.}
elements.sent-2411-500: The FPN focused on a hierarchical approach to predict attack features from multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-501: Subsequently, to enable transfer learning from fewer attack samples, they developed an unsupervised domain adaptive regularization model.
elements.sent-2411-502: They showed the potential of their approach for anti-noise performance and short running time.
elements.sent-2411-503: Their proposed framework is validated using four intrusion detection datasets used in the IoT space.
elements.sent-2411-504: In doing so, they constructed few shots datasets from the ICSX2012FS and CICIDS2017FS datasets sampling a few attack samples.

elements.sent-2411-506: Adhikari et al.~\cite{Adhikari:2017:Applying:Adaptive:Threes:Real:Time:IDS proposed a cyber-power event and intrusion detection system (EIDS) that can be used for multiclass or binary classification of traditional power system contingencies and cyber-attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-507: In doing so, they process continuous streams of high speed data from wide area monitoring systems (WAMS) and used a HAT augmented with the drift detection method (DDM) and adaptive windowing (ADWIN) that classifies traffic in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-508: HAT data stream mining is based on the idea of concept adapting very fast DTs (CVFDTs).
elements.sent-2411-509: In particular, HAT creates DTs from the data stream and updates the three after inspecting each sample.
elements.sent-2411-510: HAT does not require samples to be stored in memory as nodes in the tree hold holds rich information to perform classification.
elements.sent-2411-511: DDM compared the statistics of two windows to detect concept drifts when the number of errors increases beyond a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-512: ADWIN is a parameter free adaptive size sliding window technique that is used to detect concept change and trigger model revision.
elements.sent-2411-513: DDM and ADWIN together enhances HAT's ability to detect change and update the model.
elements.sent-2411-514: Experiments on a wide area measurement system with hardware in the loop testbed with simulated attacks showed that the combined approach of HAT, DDM, and ADWIN provides greater classification accuracy along with a small memory footprint and fast evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-515: Wang and Govindarasu~\cite{Wang:2020:Multi:Agent:Attack:Resilient:Smart:Grid presented a data-driven anomaly detection and adaptive load rejection scheme within a decentralized system integrity protection (SIP) for smart grids.}
elements.sent-2411-516: In doing so, they leveraged a SVM embedded layered DT (SVMLDT) that arrives at a decision based on the consensus among all interconnected agents.
elements.sent-2411-517: The SVMLDT works by segregating the training dataset into subsets based on all nominal features and then reducing the dimensionality of the feature space.
elements.sent-2411-518: Then SVMLDT applies a DT based SVM (DTSVM) for supervised classification.
elements.sent-2411-519: The proposed framework responds adaptively to DoS attacks by separating the multi-agent system into several interconnected subgroups so that within one subgroup, the real-time load profiles can be still shared globally.
elements.sent-2411-520: They used a real load rejection SIP scheme adopted by salt-river project to fir in the IEEE 39-bus model as a study case.
elements.sent-2411-521: Their results show that the proposed SIP can effectively detect anomalous grid operation states and then adjust its actions accordingly to adapt to the under attack situations.
elements.sent-2411-522: Camana et al.~\cite{Camana:2020:Randomized:Threes:Stealthy:Cyberattack:SG proposed an approach for cyberattack detection in smart grids based on an extremely randomized three (Extra-Trees) algorithm and kernel principal component analysis (K-PCA) for dimensionality reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-523: Extra-Trees uses a large number of DTs and chooses a split rule based on a random subset features and a partially random cut point.
elements.sent-2411-524: K-PCA is used to tackle the increasing computational complexity of big power systems by considering non-linearities inherent in data with complicated structures.
elements.sent-2411-525: They studied the attack detection problem where the labels of the samples are randomly corrupted.
elements.sent-2411-526: Specifically, a percentage of true labels in the training is flipped also know as label noise.
elements.sent-2411-527: They tested their proposed approach on the IEEE 57-bus and 118-bus systems.
elements.sent-2411-528: Their numerical results show that their approach outperforms state of the art approaches.
elements.sent-2411-529: Liao et al.~\cite{Liao:2022:Divergence:Transferability:Analysis:Adaptive:Smart:Grid:IDS proposed a divergence-based transferability analysis to decide whether or not to apply transfer learning and automatically adapt a smart grid intrusion detection strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-530: In particular, they explored three metrics to capture the similarity of data distributions to understand the relationship between detector's accuracy drop and similarity.
elements.sent-2411-531: Following up on this analysis, they trained two regression models to approximate the similarity and accuracy relationship needed to predict accuracy drops, which indicates the need for transfer learning.
elements.sent-2411-532: A domain adversarial neural network (DANN) classifier is adopted as transfer learning model.
elements.sent-2411-533: To validate their effectiveness, they used datasets from real normal operations from ISO New England~\cite{Muzhikyan:2019:SOARES:Dataset and simulated attacks from the IEEE 30-bus system in different conditions including attack timing, location, and both.}
elements.sent-2411-534: Ultimately, their approach shows that the DANN can be timely triggered to achieve an accuracy improvement over $5.0\%$.
elements.sent-2411-535: Li et al.~\cite{Li:2016:Dirichlet:Based:Detection:Smart:Grid proposed a Dirichlet-based probabilistic model to asses the reputation levels of decentralized local agents (LA).}
elements.sent-2411-536: Initial reputation levels of LAs used historical data to train the proposed model.
elements.sent-2411-537: To detect opportunistic attackers, they develop an adaptive detection algorithm with a reputation incentive mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-538: Specifically, they used the Bayes rule to assist the control center in making informed decisions about LAs being compromised.
elements.sent-2411-539: In doing so, they used a Dirichlet distribution as a prior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-540: Initial beliefs combined with a series of historical observations shape the posterior distribution that is best suited for the reputation model.
elements.sent-2411-541: To estimate the overall status of combined LA's behaviors, they leverage a reputation level in their scheme based on the graded mean value of each compliance level.
elements.sent-2411-542: They demonstrated the utility of the proposed framework using data from IEEE-39 power system using the PowerWorld simulator.
elements.sent-2411-543: Hu et al.~\cite{Hu:2022:RL:Adaptive:Feature:Boosting:SG proposed a RL-based adaptive feature boosting that leverages a series of AEs capturing critical features from multi-source smart grid data for the classification of normal, fault, and attack events.}
elements.sent-2411-544: They used multiple AEs to extract representative features from different feature sets which are extracted through a weighted feature sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-545: These extracted features are informed by a reinforcement learning approach called deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) to determine the feature sampling probability based on classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-546: AE-based extracted features are then feed in a RF classifier as base classifier along with an ensemble to distinguish between different types of incidents.
elements.sent-2411-547: They evaluated their proposed approach in two realistic datasets collected from hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) and WUSTIL-IIOT-2021 security testbeds showing an increase in classification accuracy with respect to the vanilla adaptive feature boosting.

elements.sent-2411-549: This SLR focuses on the use of AAD methods for CPS.
elements.sent-2411-550: We classify the review studies using a new taxonomy based on CPS applications and ML algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-551: This section covers the SLR findings, current approach limitations, and future research directions in AAD for CPS.
elements.sent-2411-552: Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning can be trained in both offline and online fashion on traditional ML, DL, and hybrid models.
elements.sent-2411-553: Unsupervised approaches are better equipped to detect unknown anomalies than supervised learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-554: Most unsupervised methods rely only on benign data (also know as one-class classification~\cite{ Chandola:2009:Anomaly:Detection:Survey, Yuan:2022:Trustworthy:AD:Survey ).}
elements.sent-2411-555: Semi-supervised anomaly detection typically assumes a small number of labelled normal and abnormal samples and a large number of unlabeled samples in the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-556: Therefore, we group them under the unsupervised category.
elements.sent-2411-557: We notice that a few of the reviewed papers proposed semi-supervised approaches, where the training data consist only from normal data without anomalies~\cite{Goldstein:2016:Comparative:Evaluation:Unsupervised:Anomaly:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-558: RL addresses the challenge of learning from interaction with an environment to achieve long term goals of protecting CPS against cyberattacks.
elements.sent-2411-559: Although RL is a promising approach, we noticed that it does not have widespread use in practice~\cite{Nguyen:2021:DRL:Cybersecurity .}
elements.sent-2411-560: As normal streaming data in CPS is easier to collect than attack data, unsupervised learning is a promising approach for AAD in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-561: We observe that OCSVM (a traditional ML model) and AEs (DL based) were commonly used approaches in unsupervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-562: We notice that a combination of sequential models based on RNNs such as LSTM and GRU with other DL architectures including CNNs or rule-based models have been effective on increasing detection capabilities for sophisticated attacks.
elements.sent-2411-563: As they tend to be a good fit to model the normal temporal dependencies in time series data generated by CPS, they have been used successfully in unsupervised fashion.
elements.sent-2411-564: Unsupervised models can detect a wider range of attacks including previously unseen attacks compared to supervised models at the expense of producing higher false positive rates.
elements.sent-2411-565: Generally, DL methods have achieved better performance than traditional ML methods.
elements.sent-2411-566: Nonetheless, due to expensive resource requirements and high latency, DL methods might not be the best option to deploy AAD solutions for CPS, given their usual limited resource availability.
elements.sent-2411-567: We notice that the use of hybrid and ensemble models can help to overcome the limitations of individual models by increasing the robustness of decision making.
elements.sent-2411-568: We observe that only two reviewed papers use RL strategies, suggesting there is plenty of room for exploration and promise to detect novel attacks in streaming environments.
elements.sent-2411-569: We apply our taxonomy to the reviewed work in Figure~\ref{fig:bar plots .}
elements.sent-2411-570: It reveals how the reviewed AAD for CPS work distributes across CPS applications, learning paradigm, data management, and algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-571: We make the following observations: (1) most of the reviewed work focused on ICS; this corresponds to more than 50$\%$ (24 out of 47) of the reviewed papers; (2) the vast majority of proposed literature focused on supervised models that require labels; this corresponds to three quarters (36 out of 47) of the reviewed papers; (3) most proposed methods are tested in an offline setting; this corresponds to nearly two-thirds of the reviewed papers (32 out of 47); (4) most of the proposed methods rely on DL techniques; this correspond to nearly $50\%$ (21 out of 47) of the reviewed papers.
elements.sent-2411-572: One of the most prominent findings of our analysis is the lack of online evaluation of the proposed methods with only about one third (16 out of 47) of the reviewed papers discussing it.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{images/Data_Bars.pdf} \caption{AAD for CPS distribution among application, learning paradigm, data management, and algorithm categories. } \label{fig:bar plots}
elements.sent-2411-573: Given the complexity of attacks and the conditions of the deployment environment, AAD methods are expected to use diverse techniques within resource-constrained settings.
elements.sent-2411-574: Our SLR suggests that an online unsupervised ensemble method is the most suitable approach for AAD in CPS, given the required constraints.
elements.sent-2411-575: Our SLR may potentially overlook relevant papers during the search process.
elements.sent-2411-576: While collecting AAD research papers from various publishers, there is a risk of missing those with incomplete abstracts or without adaptation focus or the CPS keyword.
elements.sent-2411-577: To address this, we used a systematic approach that combined manual searching, automatic searching, and forward and backward search to minimize the chance of missing relevant papers.
elements.sent-2411-578: We searched for AAD papers in CPS from leading engineering and science publishers that sponsor top conferences in data mining and computer security, extracting AAD and CPS keywords for manual searches.
elements.sent-2411-579: We also conducted automatic searches using a carefully selected set of keywords.
elements.sent-2411-580: To further expand our results, we applied forward and backward search snowballing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-581: Manual inspection of papers has inherent limitations and potential biases.
elements.sent-2411-582: We selected papers using a process that combined manual and automated steps, followed by validation based on quality criteria.
elements.sent-2411-583: However, the manual validation stage may be biased by the researcher's judgment, affecting the accuracy of paper quality assessment.
elements.sent-2411-584: To address this, the coauthors---experts in data mining, anomaly detection, and cybersecurity---conducted an additional in-depth review for relevance and quality screen (see Figure~\ref{fig:des-dia ).}
elements.sent-2411-585: This step aims to improve the accuracy of paper selection and reduce the risk of omissions and misclassifications.
elements.sent-2411-586: By implementing these measures, we aim to ensure the accuracy and integrity of the selected papers, minimize selection bias, and enhance the reliability of our SLR.
elements.sent-2411-587: Evaluation of AAD methods in CPS is highly dependent on the data being used.
elements.sent-2411-588: The use of low quality data with absent sophisticated attacks on CPS may lead to biased and incorrect conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-589: Some datasets are based on injecting simulated attacks under realistic conditions, which hinders nuances of CPS.
elements.sent-2411-590: Thus, it is difficult to evaluate, compare, and improve AAD without having proper datasets.
elements.sent-2411-591: Associated reasons for this limitation include: (1) cost to produce real attacks in CPS, (2) associated risk for introducing attacks in CPS, (3) the disclosure of private information~\cite{verma2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-592: Looking forward, the creation of CPS attack datasets in controlled but realistic conditions and the combination of multiple existing datasets is an interesting direction in the future.
elements.sent-2411-593: A good example is the ROAD dataset~\cite{verma2024comprehensive in the vehicle domain but other datasets are needed in other CPS applications.}
elements.sent-2411-594: As CPS transmit data in real-time, AAD techniques should act rapidly to take appropriate countermeasures in near real-time.
elements.sent-2411-595: However, most of the studies that we reviewed proposed solutions incapable of detecting attacks in near real-time.
elements.sent-2411-596: In addition, the DL-based methods we reviewed assume availability of a large number of computational resources in the cloud to arrive at conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-597: However, since CPS have multiple components, connection stability is a key factor for the deployment in cloud environments.
elements.sent-2411-598: Further AAD methods that are able to effectively process streaming data and arrive at detection decisions with low latency is a needed direction of study in the future.
elements.sent-2411-599: AAD reviewed work performed evaluation tests of their approaches on datasets from different nature, including real and synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-600: Evaluation metric comparisons using real and synthetic data are possible thanks to the common benign and attack data usually coming with the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-601: However, we notice a variety of evaluation metrics usually reported in the revised studies, so that studies do not report common metrics that allows a head-to-head comparison.
elements.sent-2411-602: Common evaluation metrics reported include accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC.
elements.sent-2411-603: In the context of anomaly detection, it is common to face imbalanced datasets, making accuracy an inappropriate metric in the area.
elements.sent-2411-604: Recent studies have suggested the use of the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) which in general is intended to address the unbalanced data issues commonly found in anomaly detection datasets~\cite{Chicco:2020:MCC:Over:F1 .}
elements.sent-2411-605: Therefore, to make fair comparisons on AAD approaches suggested metrics include precision, recall, false positive rate, false negative rate, and MCC.
elements.sent-2411-606: In addition, detection latency found in a handful of studies should be also reported particularly in the streaming context.
elements.sent-2411-607: Thus, we expect future work including suggested metrics as part of their evaluation criteria.
elements.sent-2411-608: Since attack datasets are scarce and labeling is difficult and costly, unsupervised learning techniques (e.g., clustering, OCSVM, AEs) are well suited for AAD.
elements.sent-2411-609: The fundamental assumption in unsupervised learning is that only benign data is used to model normal behavior and a threshold is used as criteria for decision making.
elements.sent-2411-610: With abundant benign data in streaming scenarios, an important future research direction is to develop adaptive threshold mechanisms that can be updated online and respond to concept drifts.
elements.sent-2411-611: Since many AAD approaches rely on DL, the lack of realistic attack and benign datasets is a common challenge.
elements.sent-2411-612: Therefore learning from a few samples in a dynamic, changing environment is key.
elements.sent-2411-613: Other fields such as computer vision and natural language processing have developed powerful machinery to learn from limited data, including transfer learning~\cite{Pan:2009:Transfer:Learning:Survey , one-shot learning~\cite{Vinyals:2016:Matching:Networks:One:Shot:Learning , and zero-shot learning~\cite{Xian:2017:Zero:Shot:Learning:Good:Bad:Ugly .}}}
elements.sent-2411-614: Building on previous work and adapting it to use small labeled datasets for AAD is an important future research direction.
elements.sent-2411-615: The reviewed papers focused mainly on the algorithmic and software aspects of their proposed AAD techniques.
elements.sent-2411-616: However, the deployment of these techniques is often overlooked due to the hardware constraints common in the CPS applications we studied.
elements.sent-2411-617: Since host-based deployment is unfeasible due to additional security and privacy related concerns, a different approach is needed so that the online conditions needed to deploy AAD are meet in a cost-effective manner.
elements.sent-2411-618: Therefore, increasing attention must be placed on the use and integration with edge, fog, or cloud computing infrastructures.
elements.sent-2411-619: To this, deployment and validation of AAD in edge-fog-cloud computing infrastructures can be considered an important future research direction.
elements.sent-2411-620: A few proposed AAD approaches are effective in identifying previously unseen anomalies in CPS with high detection rates.
elements.sent-2411-621: However, these models remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks, including white-box, black-box, and tampering attacks~\cite{Chakraborty:2021:Survey:Adversarial:Attacks:Defenses, Aloraini:2024:Adversarial:Attacks:IDS:False:Alarms .}
elements.sent-2411-622: None of the reviewed studies address how to protect the proposed AAD approaches from attacks.
elements.sent-2411-623: Therefore, developing secure AAD for CPS in adversarial settings is a promising and challenging direction for future research.~\cite{Yuan:2022:Trustworthy:AD:Survey, Mohus:2023:Adversarial:Robustness:Unsupervised:Learning .}
elements.sent-2411-624: We observe that existing solutions from the AI security domain could be adapted for AAD in CPS~\cite{Goodge:2021:Robustness:Autoencoders:AD:Adversarial:Attacks, Lo:2022:Adversarially:Robust:One:Class:Novelty:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-625: The reviewed papers primarily focus on improving classification outcomes for AAD.
elements.sent-2411-626: Most proposed methods, particularly those based on DL, emphasize black-box anomaly detection for making critical predictions.
elements.sent-2411-627: However, various stakeholders are increasingly demanding explainability.~\cite{Preece:2018:Stakeholders:Explainable:AI .}
elements.sent-2411-628: Explainable anomaly detection refers to a model's ability to clarify why and when it identifies an anomaly~\cite{Li:2023:Survey:XAD .}
elements.sent-2411-629: Prioritizing explainable AAD is a critical research direction, as decision-making in CPS demands models that can provide clear explanations of detection results without compromising prediction quality.
elements.sent-2411-630: We have performed a SLR in the field of AAD in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-631: We performed our searchers in five prominent research databases (i.e., IEEE Explore, ACM Digital Library, Emerald Insight, Springer Link, and Science Direct) and execute forward and backward snowballing search to maximize the literature search coverage.
elements.sent-2411-632: After analyzing 47 research papers and 18 review articles, this study introduces a novel taxonomy based on attacks, CPS applications, learning paradigm (i.e., supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning), and ML algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-633: In particular, we outline algorithms, datasets, attack characteristics, strengths and weaknesses developed in each of the papers.
elements.sent-2411-634: Details about the learning paradigm used are discussed with respect to each step guiding attack detection strategy across CPS applications.
elements.sent-2411-635: By using a known and standardized approach, our SLR ensures that key papers in the searched databases are found.
elements.sent-2411-636: Thus, researchers and practitioners should not need to repeat this work to find relevant publications from the period 2013 to 2023 (November).
elements.sent-2411-637: Still, the SLR approach allows the work to be repeated in the future to track field developments.
elements.sent-2411-638: Our categorization provides a clear overview of AAD in CPS and related research, making it easy to find relevant papers for specific CPS applications.
elements.sent-2411-639: The number of papers in each category shows which research areas were seen as important and challenging during the studied period.
elements.sent-2411-640: The current findings indicate that most of the previous studies investigated only a single aspect of adaptation (either data processing or model adaptation).
elements.sent-2411-641: Limited studies have provided a comprehensive overview of both aspects of AAD at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-642: Hence, this SLR contributed to the anomaly detection literature by summarizing different adaptive ML-based anomaly detection algorithms into distinct CPS applications.
elements.sent-2411-643: The intersection of these topics has not been discussed previously.
elements.sent-2411-644: The identified and categorized studies not only add the conceptual discussion in the filed of AAD but also provided several enlightened ideas for researchers and practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-645: Similarly, different types of data analysis methods can also diversify our investigation in future studies and enrich our results.
elements.sent-2411-646: Finally, based on prior research, we highlight several directions and considerations for future studies.
elements.sent-2411-647: These directions and considerations include, tight integration between near real-time data processing and an adaptive learning mode, explainable and actionable detection predictions, and quantifying the uncertainty of detection predictions.

elements.sent-2411-651: We present a comprehensive SLR, gathering 397 relevant papers and systematically analyzing 65 of them (47 research and 18 survey papers) on AAD in CPS studies from 2013 to 2023 (November).
elements.sent-2411-652: A small amount of these surveys have also explored the application of machine learning and data mining techniques to various cybersecurity domains, with an explicit focus on addressing intrusion detection challenges for securing CPS and providing insights into methodologies and best practices~\cite{buczak2015survey, olowononi2020resilient .}
elements.sent-2411-653: This section provides a summary of key concepts related to AAD in CPS.
elements.sent-2411-654: Common attack types to CPS and frequently used evaluation metrics in the detection task are also discussed in this section.
elements.sent-2411-655: A CPS contains both a physical and cyber system \cite{duo2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-656: These systems are co-designed and co-engineered from a variety of domains to be adaptive, flexible, and situationally aware \cite{li2019detection .. Edward A.}
elements.sent-2411-657: Lee~\cite{lee2015past states that the majority of CPS monitor and control the physical entities through feedback loops where physical processes affect computations.}
elements.sent-2411-658: According to Lee, CPS combines deterministic models such as differential equations and digital logic with physical systems, which creates a a new engineering discipline that requires unique models and methods.
elements.sent-2411-659: Lee points out that the terms CPS and cyberspace can be attributed to the same root, ``cybernetics", a term originated by Norbert Wiener \cite{wiener2019cybernetics .}
elements.sent-2411-660: These complex systems represent the integration of computational algorithms and physical processes that are designed to interact with embedded computers and networks.
elements.sent-2411-661: Attacks on CPS can be classified into three main categories; availability, integrity, and confidentiality \cite{duo2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-662: Availability attacks aim to block or disrupt the communication network, making data and information unavailable to legitimate users.
elements.sent-2411-663: Integrity attacks target the accuracy and trustworthiness of data and control commands of a system.
elements.sent-2411-664: Confidentiality attacks focus on unauthorized access to sensitive information, we describe them in more detail below.
elements.sent-2411-666: Denial of Service (DoS): Prohibiting of the connection between communication.
elements.sent-2411-667: These attacks aim to make the communication services unavailable through means of bombarding the system with a large number of frames \cite{rajapaksha2023ai .}
elements.sent-2411-668: Fuzzing: Attacker sends a large number of arbitrary messages into the network(s) \cite{verma2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-669: False Data Injection (FDI): Insertion of false data into a system to disrupt operations or cause incorrect responses \cite{duo2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-670: Duo et al. state that a false data injection can be identified by measuring the residuals and comparing them to a predefined threshold.
elements.sent-2411-671: Masquerade or Impersonation: An advanced form of attack, entails an attacker halting messages from a specific signal sent by a compromised node \cite{verma2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-672: In this situation, an attacker uses a fully compromised node to send fake messages at convincing frequency to imitate the target node \cite{verma2024comprehensive, rajapaksha2023ai .}
elements.sent-2411-673: Spoofing: Malicious messages are injected into network \cite{rajapaksha2023ai .}
elements.sent-2411-674: Replay: Upon hijacking a system, attackers observe and record readings for a specified amount of time and repeat those readings to distract security observation while an attack occurs \cite{mo2009secure .}
elements.sent-2411-675: Man-in-the-Middle (MitM): Attacker positions itself between two nodes to either eavesdrop or or impersonate one of the nodes and creates a false impression of normal system operation \cite{wlazlo2021man .}
elements.sent-2411-676: Eavesdropping: Confidential information stolen from eavesdropping from communications between sensors and controllers \cite{duo2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-677: For example, DoS attacks can incapacitate communication networks and cause essential services to become inaccessible \cite{suprabhath2023cyber .}
elements.sent-2411-678: Koduru, et al. highlight how DoS attacks can impact economic losses and cause widespread outages, claiming that cyber attacks on power grids are ``the most dangerous and impactful phenomenon'' \cite{suprabhath2023cyber .}
elements.sent-2411-679: Integrity attacks like false data injection can corrupt data and commands within a CPS, resulting in erroneous system behavior.
elements.sent-2411-680: Several real-life cyber-attacks are feature in the paper, we refer the reader to \cite{suprabhath2023cyber for an in-depth analysis and case-studies of impacts on CPSs from a microgrid stand point.}
elements.sent-2411-681: When false data injections are introduced to a system like smart grids and specifically operations governing traffic, consequences like service disruption and financial looses can occur \cite{humayed2017cyber .}
elements.sent-2411-682: MitM attacks can be used to flood the radio communication of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV)~\cite{dahiya2020unmanned .}
elements.sent-2411-683: UAVs are used for applications including surveillance~\cite{kim2012cyber , weather monitoring~\cite{gupta2013review , unmanned attacks \cite{gudla2018defense , disaster relief~\cite{debusk2010unmanned , rescue operations~\cite{waharte2010supporting , and many more.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-684: In the case of disaster relief, a MitM attack can intercept and alter the communication between the UAV and control station.
elements.sent-2411-685: Successfully interfering with this communication can prevent or delay the UAV from its current mission.
elements.sent-2411-686: In time sensitive missions, like disaster relief, this could delay critical aid and endanger lives~\cite{estrada2019uses .}
elements.sent-2411-687: MitM attacks can result in data breeches leading to sensitive data accessible to transnational criminal organizations or nations with adversarial relationships~\cite{li2020lightweight .}
elements.sent-2411-688: IoT is particularly susceptible to spoofing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-689: For example, robotic systems relying on a network for control can be vulnerable to various attacks that can gain access to wired/wireless communications like spoofing~\cite{yaacoub2022robotics .}
elements.sent-2411-690: In the case of FDI attacks on IoT, an attacker can exploit minor error margins tolerated by system algorithms to incrementally increase the impact of the injection which can ultimately allow the escalation of the false data to go unnoticed~\cite{bostami2019false .}
elements.sent-2411-691: Bostami et al. goes on to mention that if sensors are compromised in a home automation system, it can produce incorrect reports due to the introduction of false data~\cite{bostami2019false .}
elements.sent-2411-692: Anomaly detection refers to the task of identifying irregular behavior, also known as anomalies or outliers, based on understanding regular behavior~\cite{Chandola:2009:Anomaly:Detection:Survey .}
elements.sent-2411-693: A widely accepted definition across applications define an anomaly as an ``observation that differs so much from other observations as to arouse suspicions that it was generated by a different mechanism''~\cite{Hawkins:1980:Identification:Outliers .}
elements.sent-2411-694: The deployment of anomaly detection algorithms often prevent and deter the occurrence of critical events or even undesired critical conditions such as the case of cyberattacks~\cite{Moriano:2021:Burstiness:BGP, Moriano:2022:AHC .}
elements.sent-2411-695: Other anomaly detection applications range from different disciplines including network intrusions~\cite{Garcia:2009:Anomaly:Network:Intrusion:Detection , credit card fraud~\cite{Zhang:2022:Optimized:Anomaly:Detection:Credit:Card , tax evasion~\cite{Bolton:2001:Unsupervised:Profiling:Fraud:Detection , route hijacking~\cite{Shi:2012:Argus , and malware detection~\cite{Invernizzi:2012:Evilseed .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-696: Anomaly detection techniques start by characterizing the regular behavior of a system against which unusual patterns are compared.
elements.sent-2411-697: Characterizing regular behavior is then a prerequisite for detecting anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-698: Based on the extent to which data labels are available for detection purposes, anomaly detection is usually performed in three different ways, including supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-699: Supervised anomaly detection assumes labeled data for both normal and anomaly classes at the training stage.
elements.sent-2411-700: They focused then on building predictive models for distinguishing between both classes.
elements.sent-2411-701: Supervised anomaly detection presents two main challenges related to labeled data scarcity, namely data imbalanced and labeled data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-702: The former is related to a significantly lower amount of anomalous data versus normal data.
elements.sent-2411-703: This issue has been mitigated in the machine learning literature based on data mining techniques~\cite{Weiss:1998:Learning:Predict:Rare:Events:Sequences, Joshi:2001:Classifying:Rare:Classes:Two:Phase, Vilalta:2002:Predicting:Rare:Events:Temporal:Domains, Joshi:2002:Predicting:Rare:Classes:Boosting, Chawla:2004:Learning:Imbalanced:Datasets, Phua:2004:Minority:Report:Fraud:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-704: The former entails generating a representative proportion of data samples with anomaly labels.
elements.sent-2411-705: For this, some techniques focused on generating synthetic anomalies that are subsequently integrated with the training data~\cite{Theiler:2003:Resampling:Approach:Anomaly:Detection:Multispectral:Images, Abe:2006:Outlier:Detection:Active:Learning, Steinwart:2005:Classification:Framework:Anomaly:Detection .}
elements.sent-2411-706: Semi-supervised anomaly detection assumes there is only available normal labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-707: By relaxing the assumption about required anomaly labeled data they are usually more applicable than supervised techniques.
elements.sent-2411-708: For example, in automotive systems fault detection~\cite{Theissler:2017:Detecting:Known:Unknown:Faults:Automotive:Systems , an anomaly could represent a critical fault in a vehicle leading to an accident.}
elements.sent-2411-709: The usual way to go about using these methods is to build models using the available normal labeled data and then use it to detect anomalies in the test data.
elements.sent-2411-710: Although there are also models that use exclusively anomaly data for training purposes, it reinforces the challenge of requiring a variety of anomaly data for proper detection of a variety of anomalies~\cite{Warrender:1999:Detecting:Intrusion:System:Calls, Dasgupta:2000:Comparison:Negative:Positive:Selection, Dasgupta:2002:Anomaly:Detection:Negative:Sampling .}
elements.sent-2411-711: Unsupervised anomaly detection do not require training data making it the most popular second line of defense for detecting anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-712: The implicit assumption with these techniques is that the amount of normal labeled data is far more abundant than anomaly labeled data is the test data for reducing false positives.
elements.sent-2411-713: As the most popular alternative for performing anomaly detection, it has been adapted for detecting anomalies in a variety of applications~\cite{Goldstein:2016:Comparative:Evaluation:Unsupervised:Anomaly:Detection, Zong:2018:Deep:Autoencoding:Uncupervised:Anomaly:Detection, Hanselmann:2020:CANet, Shahriar:2023:CANshield .}
elements.sent-2411-714: Concept drift refers to an scenario where the underlying relationship between the input data $X$ and the output variable $Y$ changes over time~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}
elements.sent-2411-715: In particular, let a training sample at time $t$ be represented by the couple $\{\bm{x _{t , y_{t}\}$, which is generated by hidden joint distribution $P_{t (X, Y)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-716: Using Bayes theorem, $P_{t (X, Y)$ can be expressed as $P_{t (X, Y) = P_{t (Y | X) P_{t (X)$, where the expressions $P_{t (Y | X)$ and $P_{t (X)$ represent the posterior probability of a label given the sample and the prior distribution of the data samples.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-717: Here, the joint probability $P_{t (X, Y)$ is known as the concept.}
elements.sent-2411-718: Therefore, a concept drift happens at time $t+1$ if $P_{t (X, Y) \neq P_{t+1 (X, Y)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-719: Concept drift is very common when dealing with data streams where a data source produces data samples sequentially, in real-time, and potentially at very high speed~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-720: Concept drifts have been characterized statistically as real and virtual.
elements.sent-2411-721: On the one hand, a real concept drift happens when the posterior distribution of the labels change or $P_{t (Y | X) \neq P_{t+1 (Y | X)$ when $P_{t (X) = P_{t+1 (X)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-722: This means that the probability of a label associated with a sample has changed.
elements.sent-2411-723: On the other hand, a virtual concept drift happens when the prior distribution of the samples change or $P_{t (Y | X) = P_{t+1 (Y | X)$ when $P_{t (X) \neq P_{t+1 (X)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-724: This means that the probability of occurrence of data samples has changed.
elements.sent-2411-725: Learning algorithms are usually classified as offline and online.
elements.sent-2411-726: Offline algorithms refers to the case when the whole dataset of samples on which the algorithm is learning is available at training time.
elements.sent-2411-727: Thus, only when training is completed the model can be used for prediction.
elements.sent-2411-728: On the other hand, online algorithms learn by by processing data samples one by one without assuming to have the whole training dataset available at the beginning.
elements.sent-2411-729: In the context of adaptive anomaly detection, we focus exclusively in online algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-730: Here learning refers to the mechanisms that are used to learn from new data samples and update the prediction models.
elements.sent-2411-731: We discuss three different aspects related to learning: (1) learning mode, (2) adaptation methods, and (3) ensemble methods based on the work by Gama et al.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}
elements.sent-2411-732: Learning mode refers to the process of incorporating the knowledge of new data samples into the prediction models.
elements.sent-2411-733: Here, two main options can be operationalized: retraining and incremental.
elements.sent-2411-734: On the one hand, retraining assumes the existence of enough memory for data storage.
elements.sent-2411-735: Specifically, after training a model with the available data, whenever a new data sample arrives, the previous model is discarded.
elements.sent-2411-736: New data samples are then merged with previous data so a new model can be learned on this expanded dataset~\cite{Wu:2020:Deltagrad:Rapid:Retraining:ML, Mahadevan:2024:Cost:Aware:Retraining:ML .}
elements.sent-2411-737: On the other hand, incremental learning focuses on updating the model based on the most recent data.
elements.sent-2411-738: In doing so, algorithms process the input one-by-one and update models accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-739: Incremental learning is also used to retain knowledge acquired from previous classes~\cite{Wu:2019:Large:Scale:Incremental:Learning, Van:2022:Three:Types:Incremental:Learning .}
elements.sent-2411-740: Adaptation refers to control strategies to perform the action of adapting prediction models.
elements.sent-2411-741: Adaptation methods are usually classified as blind and informed.
elements.sent-2411-742: On the one hand, in the blind category, algorithms simply adapt models without any explicit signal suggesting to do so.
elements.sent-2411-743: Common to blind adaptation is the use of a fixed sliding window of size $\omega$ that frequently update the model by training on the last $\omega$ samples~\cite{Klinkenberg:1998:Adaptive:Information:Filtering:Learning:Concept:Drifts, Lanquillon:2001:Enhancing:Classification:Information:Filtering .}
elements.sent-2411-744: As the blind strategy is proactive, they tend to be slow when reacting to concept drifts.
elements.sent-2411-745: This is because they will forget about old concepts independent if they are happening or not.
elements.sent-2411-746: On the other hand, in the informed category, strategies are reactive depending on a signal suggesting to update the models~\cite{Bifet:2006:Kalman:Filter:Learning:Data:Streams .}
elements.sent-2411-747: Note that triggers can be based on detector's output independent of the adaptation strategy but they could also be integrated with the adaptation strategy~\cite{Gama:2006:Decision:Data:Mining:Streams ; in addition, triggers can be based on specific data descriptors~\cite{Widmer:1996:Learning:Concept:Drift:Hidden:Context .}}
elements.sent-2411-748: Ensembles combine the knowledge of multiple learners to predict outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-749: The main advantage of ensembles is the idea of creating a diversified base of knowledge using the collective wisdom of multiple learners so that learners' weaknesses can be mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-750: Predictions are then obtained by aggregating individual learners' predictions.
elements.sent-2411-751: Note that the final prediction can be obtained using different aggregation methods.
elements.sent-2411-752: Popular approaches include the idea of having learners to vote under the assumption of same vote weight.
elements.sent-2411-753: Here the prediction of the ensemble is given by the majority vote.
elements.sent-2411-754: Another popular option is is to weight votes based recent learners performance.
elements.sent-2411-755: Note that in this case, weights will evolve over time ensuring that top learner performers are assigned a higher weight.
elements.sent-2411-756: Note that in the case of steaming data the categorization of ensembles vary.
elements.sent-2411-757: Following Kuncheva's work~\cite{Kuncheva:2004:Classifier:Ensemble:Changing:Environments , ensemble classification include (1) modifying the combination rule of ensembles as concept drift happens (or dynamic combination)~\cite{Tsymbal:2006:Handling:Local:Concept:Drift ; (2) allowing base learners to update their models using latest processed data (or continuous update)~\cite{Fern:2003:Online:Ensemble:Learning, Rodriguez:2008:Combining:Classification:Changing:Environments ; (3) removing or adding learners to the ensemble (or structural update)~\cite{Street:2001:Streaming:Ensemble:Algorithm, Bouchachia:2011:Incremental:Learning:Multi:Adaptation .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-758: Data management is a critical component of modern CPS; the topics covered here pertaining to data management are collection of the data, processing of said data, and retention of data.
elements.sent-2411-759: CPS collect data from sources like sensors in real-time creating what is called a data stream.
elements.sent-2411-760: A data stream is a continuous (unbound) flow of data that generates observations in sequence and in real-time without a predefined end~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive, golab2003issues, bahri2021data .}
elements.sent-2411-761: From \cite{loeffel2017adaptive , we formalize data stream as the following.}
elements.sent-2411-762: At time $t_0$, a data stream will begin to produce unlabeled observations $\{x_{t0 , x_{t1 , x_{t2 , ...\}$, delivered sequentially at consistent time intervals ($\forall k \in \mathbb{N : t_{k+1 -t_k=u$ where $u \in \mathbb{R ^+$ is a constant) and continues indefinitely.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-763: Observation $x_t$ is corresponded by label $y_t$, Loeffel mentions that $y_t$ is assumed to always be available before the subsequent observation $x_{t+1}$, which we adopt as well.
elements.sent-2411-764: In the realm of machine learning, the objective remains consistent with traditional offline methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-765: The main task is to develop a function $h:X \to Y$ that can effectively forecast the label $y_t$ that corresponds to each observational input $x_t$~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-766: As hardware and software advances, data streams are generated from an ever increasing variety of sources.
elements.sent-2411-767: Some popular sources of data streams are IoT devices like sensor networks~\cite{golab2003issues , a wide variety of embedded systems~\cite{muller2010data like ECUs~\cite{verma2024comprehensive , computer network traffic like data packets and logs~\cite{gaber2005mining .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-768: Data stream management systems must handle the unique characteristics of data streams and continuous queries by supporting order-based and time-based operations, such as queries over moving windows~\cite{golab2003issues .}
elements.sent-2411-769: Due to the inability to store complete streams, these systems often use approximate summary structures (synopses or digests) which may not provide exact answers, and they avoid blocking operators to ensure timely results~\cite{golab2003issues .}
elements.sent-2411-770: We can see some of these requirement present in real-world applications like wireless sensor networks where numerous nodes with limited computing and communication capabilities collaborate to perform tasks such as data acquisition or event detection~\cite{muller2010data .}
elements.sent-2411-771: Bahri et al.~\cite{bahri2021data state that data streams mining faces several common challenges across tasks, possibly the most important is dealing with ever evolving data streams that deliver instances quickly and require scalable algorithms capable of processing data dynamically.}
elements.sent-2411-772: Loeffel et al.~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive claim that online learning algorithms are essential for handling sequential observations and allow models to continuously update with new data points without access to the entire dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-773: These algorithms must also efficiently manage constrained memory due to the impractically of storing all past observations for potentially infinite data streams.
elements.sent-2411-774: Gama et al.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift state that a major challenge in data streams is concept drift where changes in data distribution require adaptive algorithms to maintain accuracy and relevance.}
elements.sent-2411-775: In this context, memory management involves updating predictive models with new information while forgetting outdated data.
elements.sent-2411-776: Short-term memory deals with which data to learn from and how to discard old data which utilizes the assumption that recent data is most informative.
elements.sent-2411-777: To handle the demanding processing requirements of modern data streams, innovative processing techniques have been employed throughout literature.
elements.sent-2411-778: Gama et al. also mention that online learning algorithms typically use single-example memory and processes new observations in sequence while updating the model as needed.
elements.sent-2411-779: These methods typically lack explicit forgetting mechanics and may adapt slowly to abrupt changes.
elements.sent-2411-780: Multiple-example memory approaches (sliding windows, etc.) maintain a set of recent examples to build models that balance fast adaptation with stability through fixed or variable window lengths.
elements.sent-2411-781: A crucial element in data streams is the forgetting mechanism used for processing evolving data and discarding outdated observations for model relevance.
elements.sent-2411-782: Gama et al.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift outline two main forgetting mechanisms, abrupt and gradual.}
elements.sent-2411-783: Abrupt forgetting uses sliding windows to keep only recent data and gradual forgetting retains all data but decreases the importance of older observations using a weighting system.
elements.sent-2411-784: Gradual forgetting uses an aggregation function $G(X,S)$ and newer statistics are computed as $S=G(X_i,\alpha_{i-1 )$, where $\alpha \in (0,1)$~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift is a fading factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-785: To evaluate performance of learning algorithms within the data stream environment, appropriate performance evaluations and metrics must be selected in coordination with the learning task's objective.
elements.sent-2411-786: It is particularly important to determine which observations are used for training and testing~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}
elements.sent-2411-787: Loeffel~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive mentions that traditional cross-validation isn't suitable for evolving data streams due to temporal order dependencies.}
elements.sent-2411-788: Two common methods are used to evaluate time-ordered data; Holdout and Prequential~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive, gama2009issues, Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift, cerqueira2020evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-789: Holdout method works by training a model on a set of observations and testing on a subsequent observations at regular intervals~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-790: Here, the algorithm is trained with a set of observations $\{t_1, \ldots, t_k\}$ which produces a model $h_{t_k}$.
elements.sent-2411-791: This is then tested on a subsequent set $\{t_{k+1 , \ldots, t_{k+l}\}$~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}}
elements.sent-2411-792: Next, the model is updated with the next set of observations $\{t_{k+1 , \ldots, t_2k\}$ which results in a new model $h_{t_{2k }$ and is tested again on $\{t_{2k+1 , \ldots, t_{2k+l}\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-793: This entire procedure repeats until all observations are used~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-794: Prequential evaluation tests new observations before using its label for training (updating the model) which allows for continuous performance assessment~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-795: Three prequential evaluation methods are typically used; landmark window (Interleaved Test-Then-Train), sliding windows, and forgetting mechanisms~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-796: The landmark window error is computed as the average of all prediction errors at time $T$ since the beginning of the streaming data given as $L_T=\frac{1 {T}\sum_{t=1 ^n\ell(y_t, \hat{y _t)$~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-797: The fading factor technique applies a decay factor, $\alpha$, to previous errors.
elements.sent-2411-798: This technique offers tracking of recent performance changes using the formula $L_T=\frac{S_T {N_T}$ where, $S_T=\ell_t +\alpha S_{T-1}$ and $N_T=1 +\alpha N_{T-1}$~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}}
elements.sent-2411-799: Finally, sliding windows evaluate performance over the latest $k$ observations expressed as $L_T=\frac{1 {k}\sum_{t=T-k+1 ^T \ell_t$~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-800: This highlights algorithm reactivity to concept changes similar to fading factor~\cite{loeffel2017adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-801: Traditional metrics are used like precision, recall, and sensitivity for accuracy measures with metrics like root mean square error (RMSE) and mean absolute scaled errors for regression objectives.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}
elements.sent-2411-802: Baseline approaches like moving average used during prediction of time-series are especially important for comparing the effectiveness of intelligent adaptive techniques against simple predictions.
elements.sent-2411-803: Streaming settings to consider are the computational cost (RAM-hours) and class imbalance (kappa statistic).
elements.sent-2411-804: Gama et al.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift mention that change detection performance is evaluated using measures like the probability of true change detection, probability of false alarms, and detection delay.}
elements.sent-2411-805: Utilizing these metrics during inference can help researchers or practitioners assess the impact of change detection on the overall performance of adaptive models especially with known or synthetic drifts.
elements.sent-2411-806: When evaluating adaptive learning techniques across multiple aligned data streams, it is essential to apply and estimate the performance of these models on individual but related data streams using methods like cross-validation~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}
elements.sent-2411-807: This approach is particularly useful for models applied across similar feature spaces and allows for parameter tuning and validation across subsets of streams.
elements.sent-2411-808: Statistical significance is important when comparing classifiers with many researchers and practitioners using the McNemar test to assess differences in error rates between two classifiers.
elements.sent-2411-809: The McNemar statistic $M$ is given as: $M=sign(a-b) \times \frac{(a-b)^2 {a+b}$, where $a$ is the number of instances misclassified by the first classifier and correctly classified by the second, and $b$ represents the number of instances where the second classifier misclassifies data that the first classifier correctly classifies.~\cite{Gama:2014:Survey:Concept:Drift .}}
elements.sent-2411-810: The McNemar test follows the $\chi^2$ distribution.
elements.sent-2411-811: When more than two classifiers are used, Gama et al. point to the Nemenyi test that evaluates performance differences using average ranks across datasets which determines significant differences with a critical difference (CD) formula: $CD=q_\alpha \sqrt{\frac{k(k+1) {6N }$, where the number of learners is $k$, the number of datasets is $N$, and $q_\alpha$ are the critical values.}}

elements.sent-2411-815: While surveying the literature, it was observed that studies commonly sorted applications based on certain themes.
